abstract:
The advancement of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning has significantly
enhanced the capabilities of large language models (LLMs) and large
vision-language models (LVLMs). However, a rigorous evaluation framework for
video CoT reasoning remains absent. Current video benchmarks fail to adequately
assess the reasoning process and expose whether failures stem from deficiencies
in perception or reasoning capabilities. Therefore, we introduce VCR-Bench, a
novel benchmark designed to comprehensively evaluate LVLMs' Video
Chain-of-Thought Reasoning capabilities. VCR-Bench comprises 859 videos
spanning a variety of video content and durations, along with 1,034
high-quality question-answer pairs. Each pair is manually annotated with a
stepwise CoT rationale, where every step is tagged to indicate its association
with the perception or reasoning capabilities. Furthermore, we design seven
distinct task dimensions and propose the CoT score to assess the entire CoT
process based on the stepwise tagged CoT rationals. Extensive experiments on
VCR-Bench highlight substantial limitations in current LVLMs. Even the
top-performing model, o1, only achieves a 62.8% CoT score and an 56.7%
accuracy, while most models score below 40%. Experiments show most models score
lower on perception than reasoning steps, revealing LVLMs' key bottleneck in
temporal-spatial information processing for complex video reasoning. A robust
positive correlation between the CoT score and accuracy confirms the validity
of our evaluation framework and underscores the critical role of CoT reasoning
in solving complex video reasoning tasks. We hope VCR-Bench to serve as a
standardized evaluation framework and expose the actual drawbacks in complex
video reasoning task.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Visual perception, not reasoning, is the primary bottleneck for video understanding models
Strong positive correlation (r=0.89) exists between CoT scores and answer accuracy
Models tend to omit reasoning steps rather than make incorrect inferences
originalProblem: Existing video benchmarks focus only on final answer accuracy, neglecting evaluation of the reasoning process
Current evaluation methods cannot distinguish between perception and reasoning failures in video understanding models
results: Top performing model achieved 62.8% CoT score and 56.7% accuracy
Average perception scores (33.5) were lower than reasoning scores (42.5)
Temporal-spatial grounding tasks proved most challenging for current models
Larger models with better instruction-following capabilities achieved higher CoT scores
solution: Created a comprehensive benchmark with manually annotated stepwise CoT rationales
Developed a multi-dimensional evaluation framework that separately assesses perception and reasoning capabilities
Introduced CoT scoring methodology based on precision and recall of reasoning steps
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from USTC and Huawei Noah's Ark Lab introduce VCR-Bench, an evaluation framework for video Chain-of-Thought reasoning that includes 859 videos with 1,034 annotated question-answer pairs across seven task dimensions, revealing significant limitations in current Large Vision Language Models with even top performers achieving only 62.8% CoT scores.

title: VCR-Bench: A Comprehensive Evaluation Framework for Video  Chain-of-Thought Reasoning

abstract:
Existing evaluation frameworks for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs)
primarily focus on image reasoning or general video understanding tasks,
largely overlooking the significant role of image context in video
comprehension. To bridge this gap, we propose IV-Bench, the first comprehensive
benchmark for evaluating Image-Grounded Video Perception and Reasoning.
IV-Bench consists of 967 videos paired with 2,585 meticulously annotated
image-text queries across 13 tasks (7 perception and 6 reasoning tasks) and 5
representative categories. Extensive evaluations of state-of-the-art
open-source (e.g., InternVL2.5, Qwen2.5-VL) and closed-source (e.g., GPT-4o,
Gemini2-Flash and Gemini2-Pro) MLLMs demonstrate that current models
substantially underperform in image-grounded video Perception and Reasoning,
merely achieving at most 28.9% accuracy. Further analysis reveals key factors
influencing model performance on IV-Bench, including inference pattern, frame
number, and resolution. Additionally, through a simple data synthesis approach,
we demonstratethe challenges of IV- Bench extend beyond merely aligning the
data format in the training proecss. These findings collectively provide
valuable insights for future research. Our codes and data are released in
this https URL

keyInsights: Larger MLLMs benefit from image contexts, especially when the image is presented after video frames, suggesting input order influences information integration.
Performance consistently improves with an increased number of frames, emphasizing the importance of temporal information for video understanding.

Simple data synthesis yielded only marginal performance gains, suggesting that current MLLM limitations in image-grounded video understanding are not merely due to a lack of data in that format, but indicate a deeper architectural or reasoning gap.
originalProblem: Existing Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) benchmarks primarily focus on either image-based reasoning or general video understanding with text-only queries.
A critical gap exists in evaluating MLLMs' ability to leverage static image context as an essential cue for interpreting dynamic video content.
Current benchmarks often allow questions to be answered without the true indispensability of all modalities (image, video, and text).
results: State-of-the-art MLLMs achieve only 27-28% overall accuracy on IV-Bench, remaining moderately above the 10% random guessing baseline, highlighting significant room for improvement.
Perception tasks are generally easier than reasoning tasks; temporal reasoning is particularly challenging, with top models achieving only 16.7% accuracy.
Increasing model scale provides only moderate performance improvements, which are more pronounced in perception-based tasks than in complex reasoning tasks.
solution: Introduces IV-Bench, the first comprehensive benchmark designed for image-grounded video perception and reasoning in MLLMs.
Comprises 967 diverse videos paired with 2,585 meticulously annotated image-text queries, where an external image provides indispensable contextual cues.
Features 13 distinct evaluation tasks (7 perception, 6 reasoning) and employs a rigorous two-stage quality control process to ensure the necessity of all modalities for answering questions.
paper_summary.summary: IV-Bench, a new benchmark by M-A-P and ByteDance, evaluates Multimodal Large Language Models on their ability to integrate static image context with dynamic video content for perception and reasoning. Evaluations reveal that state-of-the-art MLLMs achieve only moderate accuracy, indicating significant limitations in this specific multimodal understanding capability.

title: IV-Bench: A Benchmark for Image-Grounded Video Perception and Reasoning  in Multimodal LLMs

abstract:
Video generation assessment is essential for ensuring that generative models
produce visually realistic, high-quality videos while aligning with human
expectations. Current video generation benchmarks fall into two main
categories: traditional benchmarks, which use metrics and embeddings to
evaluate generated video quality across multiple dimensions but often lack
alignment with human judgments; and large language model (LLM)-based
benchmarks, though capable of human-like reasoning, are constrained by a
limited understanding of video quality metrics and cross-modal consistency. To
address these challenges and establish a benchmark that better aligns with
human preferences, this paper introduces Video-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark
featuring a rich prompt suite and extensive evaluation dimensions. This
benchmark represents the first attempt to systematically leverage MLLMs across
all dimensions relevant to video generation assessment in generative models. By
incorporating few-shot scoring and chain-of-query techniques, Video-Bench
provides a structured, scalable approach to generated video evaluation.
Experiments on advanced models including Sora demonstrate that Video-Bench
achieves superior alignment with human preferences across all dimensions.
Moreover, in instances where our framework's assessments diverge from human
evaluations, it consistently offers more objective and accurate insights,
suggesting an even greater potential advantage over traditional human judgment.

keyInsights: MLLMs can achieve superior alignment with human judgment in video generation evaluation when provided with structured prompting techniques.

The "Chain-of-Query" approach effectively transforms complex video content into detailed textual descriptions and iterative questions, significantly improving MLLMs' cross-modal understanding for alignment.
"Few-shot Scoring" enables MLLMs to provide more calibrated and nuanced ratings for subjective video quality dimensions by leveraging comparative multimodal contexts.
originalProblem: The rapid advancement of text-to-video (T2V) models necessitates a sophisticated and reliable evaluation benchmark.
Existing metric-based benchmarks often fail to align with nuanced human judgments regarding video quality and fidelity.
Prior LLM-based methods struggle with accurate cross-modal comparison for video-condition alignment and precise, fine-grained scoring for subjective video quality aspects.
results: Video-Bench achieved the highest Spearman's rank correlation with human ratings across all dimensions, improving Video-Condition Alignment by 0.093 over CompBench.
The MLLM-based evaluations demonstrated a Krippendorff’s α agreement of 0.50 with human scores, aligning closely with inter-rater human agreement (0.52).
Ablation studies showed that "Chain-of-Query" and "Few-shot Scoring" are critical, with FSS improving human alignment in video quality dimensions by an average of 10.33%.
solution: Developed an MLLM-centric evaluation framework, Video-Bench, to systematically assess video generation across comprehensive quality and alignment dimensions.

Introduced "Chain-of-Query" (CoQ), an iterative prompting method where an MLLM describes video, generates targeted questions, and answers them to enhance cross-modal comparison for video-condition alignment.
Implemented "Few-shot Scoring" (FSS) to calibrate MLLM judgments for subjective video quality, using other generated videos from the same prompt as comparative references to achieve nuanced scores.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Stanford University, and the National University of Singapore, along with collaborators, developed Video-Bench, the first systematic multimodal large language model (MLLM)-centric benchmark for evaluating video generation models. It employs "Chain-of-Query" and "Few-shot Scoring" techniques to achieve superior alignment with human preferences, improving Video-Condition Alignment by 0.093 over prior methods and demonstrating strong robustness.

title: Video-Bench: Human-Aligned Video Generation Benchmark

abstract:
With the rapid development of Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs), a
number of diagnostic benchmarks have recently emerged to evaluate the
comprehension capabilities of these models. However, most benchmarks
predominantly assess spatial understanding in the static image tasks, while
overlooking temporal understanding in the dynamic video tasks. To alleviate
this issue, we introduce a comprehensive Multi-modal Video understanding
Benchmark, namely MVBench, which covers 20 challenging video tasks that cannot
be effectively solved with a single frame. Specifically, we first introduce a
novel static-to-dynamic method to define these temporal-related tasks. By
transforming various static tasks into dynamic ones, we enable the systematic
generation of video tasks that require a broad spectrum of temporal skills,
ranging from perception to cognition. Then, guided by the task definition, we
automatically convert public video annotations into multiple-choice QA to
evaluate each task. On one hand, such a distinct paradigm allows us to build
MVBench efficiently, without much manual intervention. On the other hand, it
guarantees evaluation fairness with ground-truth video annotations, avoiding
the biased scoring of LLMs. Moreover, we further develop a robust video MLLM
baseline, i.e., VideoChat2, by progressive multi-modal training with diverse
instruction-tuning data. The extensive results on our MVBench reveal that, the
existing MLLMs are far from satisfactory in temporal understanding, while our
VideoChat2 largely surpasses these leading models by over 15% on MVBench. All
models and data are available at https://github.com/OpenGVLab/Ask-Anything.

organization_info.name: State Key Laboratory for Novel Software Technology, Nanjing University

keyInsights: Existing state-of-the-art multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) perform poorly (around 30-35% accuracy) on temporal video understanding tasks, indicating a significant gap in their ability to leverage temporal information.
A strong spatio-temporal visual encoder, diverse and video-centric instruction-tuning data, and progressive multi-modal training are critical for improving MLLMs' temporal reasoning capabilities.
A simple, effective prompt design using 'Best Option: (' can guarantee 100% extraction accuracy for multiple-choice answers, improving evaluation fairness and reliability.
originalProblem: Most existing benchmarks for multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) are image-centric, primarily evaluating spatial understanding in static images and overlooking crucial temporal comprehension in videos.
Prior video-centric MLLM evaluation efforts are often limited to basic tasks, specific domains, or restricted scenes, lacking comprehensive coverage for open-world scenarios.
Current video benchmarks often rely on labor-intensive manual annotations, making them costly and difficult to scale, and open-ended answer evaluations can introduce biases.
results: Existing MLLMs (e.g., VideoChat, VideoLLaMA) achieve low average accuracy (around 30-35%) on MVBench, demonstrating their limited temporal understanding and minimal improvement over text-only baselines.
VideoChat2 significantly outperforms leading MLLMs and GPT-4V, achieving an average accuracy of 51.1% on MVBench, and further outperforming GPT-4V by 16.9% with a Mistral-based LLM.

Ablation studies confirm the importance of visual encoder choice (UMT-L outperforms EVA-CLIP-g), data diversity, and progressive multi-modal training for enhancing temporal understanding, with LoRA consistently improving results.
solution: Introduces MVBench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating multi-modal large language models' (MLLMs) temporal understanding in videos, featuring 20 diverse temporal tasks derived using a 'static-to-dynamic' method.

Develops an automatic question-answering generation pipeline leveraging 11 public video datasets and large language models (LLMs) to create multiple-choice questions, ensuring efficiency and unbiased scoring.

Presents VideoChat2, a robust video MLLM baseline trained with 2 million diverse instruction-tuning samples and a three-stage progressive multi-modal training paradigm to enhance spatio-temporal reasoning.
paper_summary.summary: This paper introduces MVBench, a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate the temporal comprehension capabilities of multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) in dynamic video tasks. It also develops VideoChat2, a new video MLLM baseline that significantly surpasses existing models, including GPT-4V, on MVBench and other video understanding benchmarks.

title: MVBench: A Comprehensive Multi-modal Video Understanding Benchmark

abstract:
With the rapid development of multimodal models, the demand for assessing
video understanding capabilities has been steadily increasing. However,
existing benchmarks for evaluating video understanding exhibit significant
limitations in coverage, task diversity, and scene adaptability. These
shortcomings hinder the accurate assessment of models' comprehensive video
understanding capabilities. To tackle this challenge, we propose a hierarchical
and holistic video understanding (H2VU) benchmark designed to evaluate both
general video and online streaming video comprehension. This benchmark
contributes three key features:
Extended video duration: Spanning videos from brief 3-second clips to
comprehensive 1.5-hour recordings, thereby bridging the temporal gaps found in
current benchmarks. Comprehensive assessment tasks: Beyond traditional
perceptual and reasoning tasks, we have introduced modules for
countercommonsense comprehension and trajectory state tracking. These additions
test the models' deep understanding capabilities beyond mere prior knowledge.
Enriched video data: To keep pace with the rapid evolution of current AI
agents, we have expanded first-person streaming video datasets. This expansion
allows for the exploration of multimodal models' performance in understanding
streaming videos from a first-person perspective. Extensive results from H2VU
reveal that existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) possess
substantial potential for improvement in our newly proposed evaluation tasks.
We expect that H2VU will facilitate advancements in video understanding
research by offering a comprehensive and in-depth analysis of MLLMs.

keyInsights: The benchmark's hierarchical structure, categorizing tasks from fundamental perception to advanced reasoning, provides a granular assessment of MLLM capabilities.

A rigorous dataset purification process, including static scene filtering, dialogue content recognition, and knowledge dependency removal, ensures that evaluation tasks genuinely require cross-modal understanding rather than relying on prior textual knowledge.
The deliberate inclusion of first-person streaming video data highlights the importance of real-time, sequential processing and decision-making for MLLMs in interactive, agent-based scenarios.
originalProblem: Current video understanding benchmarks for MLLMs suffer from limited temporal scope, often focusing on short clips that do not represent diverse real-world video durations.
Existing benchmarks inadequately evaluate complex reasoning and dynamic state tracking, frequently allowing MLLMs to rely on intrinsic prior knowledge rather than deep video-centric understanding.

There is a notable lack of real-world scenario adaptability in most benchmarks, which predominantly use third-person video data and neglect the unique challenges of first-person streaming content relevant for AI agents.
results: State-of-the-art MLLMs, including Gemini-1.5-Pro (72.5%) and leading open-source models like Qwen2.5-VL (69.9%), were evaluated, with open-source options showing competitive overall performance.

All evaluated models exhibited significantly lower performance on the new Countercommonsense Comprehension tasks (e.g., Qwen2.5-VL at 62.37%) and State Trajectory Tracking tasks (e.g., InternVL2.5 at 52.84%), indicating a struggle with deep temporal reasoning and reliance on prior knowledge.

A notable performance drop was observed for many models when evaluating online streaming video scenarios, suggesting insufficient optimization for continuous, real-time video understanding from first-person perspectives.
solution: The H²VU-Benchmark introduces a comprehensive, multi-faceted evaluation system with a three-tier hierarchical competency classification, spanning 10,183 QA tasks across diverse video durations from 3 seconds to 1.5 hours.

It features two novel task modules, Countercommonsense Comprehension and State Trajectory Tracking, specifically designed to assess deep video-centric reasoning and continuous entity tracking, reducing reliance on MLLMs' prior knowledge.

The benchmark incorporates a substantial newly collected dataset of first-person streaming videos, alongside purified general video data, to evaluate MLLMs' real-world adaptability for AI agent scenarios.
paper_summary.summary: The H²VU-Benchmark provides a new comprehensive evaluation framework for Multimodal Large Language Models' video understanding capabilities, integrating diverse video durations, novel countercommonsense and state trajectory tracking tasks, and first-person streaming content. Evaluations reveal that current MLLMs significantly underperform on these advanced reasoning and tracking tasks compared to traditional perception and reasoning.

title: H2VU-Benchmark: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Hierarchical Holistic  Video Understanding

abstract:
In the quest for artificial general intelligence, Multi-modal Large Language
Models (MLLMs) have emerged as a focal point in recent advancements. However,
the predominant focus remains on developing their capabilities in static image
understanding. The potential of MLLMs in processing sequential visual data is
still insufficiently explored, highlighting the absence of a comprehensive,
high-quality assessment of their performance. In this paper, we introduce
Video-MME, the first-ever full-spectrum, Multi-Modal Evaluation benchmark of
MLLMs in Video analysis. Our work distinguishes from existing benchmarks
through four key features: 1) Diversity in video types, spanning 6 primary
visual domains with 30 subfields to ensure broad scenario generalizability; 2)
Duration in temporal dimension, encompassing both short-, medium-, and
long-term videos, ranging from 11 seconds to 1 hour, for robust contextual
dynamics; 3) Breadth in data modalities, integrating multi-modal inputs besides
video frames, including subtitles and audios, to unveil the all-round
capabilities of MLLMs; 4) Quality in annotations, utilizing rigorous manual
labeling by expert annotators to facilitate precise and reliable model
assessment. 900 videos with a total of 254 hours are manually selected and
annotated by repeatedly viewing all the video content, resulting in 2,700
question-answer pairs. With Video-MME, we extensively evaluate various
state-of-the-art MLLMs, including GPT-4 series and Gemini 1.5 Pro, as well as
open-source image models like InternVL-Chat-V1.5 and video models like
LLaVA-NeXT-Video. Our experiments reveal that Gemini 1.5 Pro is the
best-performing commercial model, significantly outperforming the open-source
models. Our dataset along with these findings underscores the need for further
improvements in handling longer sequences and multi-modal data. Project Page:
this https URL

keyInsights: Long video understanding remains a significant challenge for MLLMs, with performance consistently declining as video duration increases, even for advanced models.
Integrating additional modalities like subtitles and audio significantly enhances MLLM performance, particularly for longer videos and those requiring domain-specific knowledge.
Commercial MLLMs currently demonstrate a substantial lead over open-source models in video comprehension, indicating areas for focused research in the open-source community.
originalProblem: Current Multi-modal Large Language Model (MLLM) evaluation primarily focuses on static images, neglecting the dynamic and temporal nature of real-world video.

Existing video-based MLLM benchmarks lack diversity in video types, sufficient temporal coverage (often focusing on short clips), and rarely incorporate all relevant modalities (e.g., subtitles, audio).

There is a need for a robust and comprehensive framework to assess MLLMs' capabilities in processing complex, dynamic visual information and guide their development towards Artificial General Intelligence (AGI).
results: Gemini 1.5 Pro achieved the highest accuracy on Video-MME (75% with frames, 81.3% with subtitles), outperforming other commercial models like GPT-4o and open-source alternatives.
Open-source MLLMs exhibited a substantial performance gap compared to commercial models, with the leading open-source model VILA-1.5 achieving 59% accuracy.

Performance across all MLLMs consistently declined with increased video duration (e.g., Gemini 1.5 Pro dropped 14.3% from short to long videos), and integrating subtitles and audio consistently improved accuracy, especially for longer videos.
solution: Researchers from Nanjing University and collaborators introduced Video-MME, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating MLLMs in video analysis, covering diverse domains and temporal lengths from 2 to 60 minutes.
The benchmark integrates multimodal data, including video frames, subtitles, and audio tracks, to assess MLLMs' ability to process sequential and complementary information.

Video-MME includes 2,700 high-quality, multiple-choice question-answer pairs spanning 12 task types, meticulously annotated by experts and filtered to ensure questions require genuine video understanding.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Nanjing University, CASIA, HKU, and other institutions introduced Video-MME, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) in video analysis, covering diverse domains, temporal lengths, and modalities. Evaluations on Video-MME revealed that commercial MLLMs, particularly Gemini 1.5 Pro, achieved higher accuracy than open-source models, and performance across all models declined with increasing video duration, despite gains from integrating subtitles and audio.

title: Video-MME: The First-Ever Comprehensive Evaluation Benchmark of  Multi-modal LLMs in Video Analysis

abstract: We introduce MAVERIX (Multimodal audiovisual Evaluation and Recognition IndeX), a unified benchmark to probe the video understanding in multimodal LLMs, encompassing video, audio, text inputs with human performance baselines. Although recent advancements in models with vision and audio understanding capabilities have shown substantial progress, the field lacks a standardized evaluation framework to thoroughly assess their cross-modality comprehension performance. MAVERIX curates 2,556 questions from 700 videos, in the form of both multiple-choice and open-ended formats, explicitly designed to evaluate multimodal models through questions that necessitate tight integration of video and audio information, spanning a broad spectrum of agentic scenarios. MAVERIX uniquely provides models with audiovisual questions, closely mimicking the multimodal perceptual experiences available to humans during inference and decision-making processes. To our knowledge, MAVERIX is the first benchmark aimed explicitly at assessing comprehensive audiovisual integration in such granularity. Experiments with state-of-the-art models, including Qwen 2.5 Omni and Gemini 2.5 Flash-Lite, show performance around 64% accuracy, while human experts reach near-ceiling performance of 92.8%, exposing a substantial gap to human-level comprehension. With standardized evaluation protocols, a rigorously annotated pipeline, and a public toolkit, MAVERIX establishes a challenging testbed for advancing audiovisual multimodal intelligence.
title: MAVERIX: Multimodal Audio-Visual Evaluation and Recognition IndeX

abstract: The evaluation of Long Video Understanding (LVU) performance poses an important but challenging research problem. Despite previous efforts, the existing video understanding benchmarks are severely constrained by several issues, especially the insufficient lengths of videos, a lack of diversity in video types and evaluation tasks, and the inappropriateness for evaluating LVU performances. To address the above problems, we propose a new benchmark called MLVU (Multi-task Long Video Understanding Benchmark) for the comprehensive and in-depth evaluation of LVU. MLVU presents the following critical values: \textit{1)} The substantial and flexible extension of video lengths, which enables the benchmark to evaluate LVU performance across a wide range of durations. \textit{2)} The inclusion of various video genres, e.g., movies, surveillance footage, egocentric videos, cartoons, game videos, etc., which reflects the models' LVU performances in different scenarios. \textit{3)} The development of diversified evaluation tasks, which enables a comprehensive examination of MLLMs' key abilities in long-video understanding. The empirical study with 23 latest MLLMs reveals significant room for improvement in today's technique, as all existing methods struggle with most of the evaluation tasks and exhibit severe performance degradation when handling longer videos. Additionally, it suggests that factors such as context length, image-understanding ability, and the choice of LLM backbone can play critical roles in future advancements. We anticipate that MLVU will advance the research of long video understanding by providing a comprehensive and in-depth analysis of MLLMs.

organization_info.name: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

keyInsights: Current MLLMs, including leading proprietary models like GPT-4o, demonstrate significant room for improvement in long video understanding, particularly struggling with multi-detail and fine-grained temporal reasoning tasks.
Performance of MLLMs consistently declines as video length increases, highlighting a critical limitation in processing extended contexts.
Long Video Understanding performance is strongly correlated with context length, foundational image understanding ability, and the scale/advancement of the underlying LLM backbone.
originalProblem: Existing video understanding benchmarks primarily use short video clips, failing to assess MLLMs' ability to comprehend long-term dependencies and narrative arcs in extended video sequences.
Current benchmarks lack diversity in video types and tasks, providing an incomplete evaluation of MLLMs' generalizability across various real-world scenarios and cognitive demands.
Many 'long video' tasks in prior benchmarks can be solved without genuine temporal understanding (e.g., from single frames or common knowledge), preventing a true assessment of MLLMs' capabilities.
results: GPT-4o achieved an average score of 54.5% on multiple-choice tasks and a G-Avg of 5.87 (out of 10) on generation tasks, significantly underperforming human baselines.
Recent open-source long video MLLMs (e.g., LLaVA-Onevision) have made substantial progress, with LLaVA-Onevision trailing GPT-4o by only 2.8% in M-Avg, narrowing the gap with proprietary models.

Models showed consistent performance degradation on the 'Time-ladder' dataset as video length increased from 180s to 600s, with short video models approaching random chance levels at longer durations.
solution: A benchmark (MLVU) was constructed using 1,730 videos ranging from 3 minutes to over 2 hours, with an average length of 15 minutes, allowing for flexible evaluation across incremental segments.

Nine distinct evaluation tasks were developed, categorized into Holistic, Single-Detail, and Multi-Detail LVU, covering reasoning, captioning, recognition, perception, and summarization across diverse genres like movies, surveillance, and egocentric videos.

Tasks are specifically designed to necessitate deep temporal understanding and avoid shortcut solutions, requiring MLLMs to integrate information across extended sequences and accurately locate/infer details within long videos.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from BAAI and several top Chinese universities introduce MLVU, a multi-task long video understanding benchmark designed to rigorously evaluate Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) by utilizing videos from 3 minutes to over 2 hours and featuring 9 diverse tasks. The benchmark reveals that even leading MLLMs like GPT-4o achieve only moderate performance (54.5% average on multiple-choice tasks), indicating significant room for improvement in handling extended temporal reasoning.

title: MLVU: Benchmarking Multi-task Long Video Understanding

abstract: Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) have achieved remarkable progress across various capabilities; however, complex video reasoning in the scientific domain remains a significant and challenging frontier. Current video benchmarks predominantly target general scenarios where perception/recognition is heavily relied on, while with relatively simple reasoning tasks, leading to saturation and thus failing to effectively evaluate advanced multimodal cognitive skills. To address this critical gap, we introduce SciVideoBench, a rigorous benchmark specifically designed to assess advanced video reasoning in scientific contexts. SciVideoBench consists of 1,000 carefully crafted multiple-choice questions derived from cutting-edge scientific experimental videos spanning over 25 specialized academic subjects and verified by a semi-automatic system. Each question demands sophisticated domain-specific knowledge, precise spatiotemporal perception, and intricate logical reasoning, effectively challenging models' higher-order cognitive abilities. Our evaluation highlights significant performance deficits in state-of-the-art proprietary and open-source LMMs, including Gemini 2.5 Pro and Qwen2.5-VL, indicating substantial room for advancement in video reasoning capabilities. Detailed analyses of critical factors such as reasoning complexity and visual grounding provide valuable insights and clear direction for future developments in LMMs, driving the evolution of truly capable multimodal AI co-scientists. We hope SciVideoBench could fit the interests of the community and help to push the boundary of cutting-edge AI for border science.

organization_info.name: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

keyInsights: Current state-of-the-art LMMs demonstrate significant deficiencies in scientific video reasoning, with top models achieving scores considerably lower than human experts and indicating the indispensable role of visual information.

Quantitative reasoning tasks consistently present the greatest challenge for LMMs, underscoring a particular weakness in precise numerical perception and multi-step calculation within complex visual contexts.

Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting substantially improves proprietary LMM performance, especially in quantitative reasoning, by scaffolding complex multi-step processes, but yields inconsistent benefits for open-source models in abstract reasoning.
originalProblem: Existing video benchmarks primarily focus on general domains and simple reasoning tasks, leading to performance saturation among state-of-the-art LMMs.

Prior scientific and educational video benchmarks often contain college-level content or allow LMMs to succeed without deep visual understanding, failing to challenge advanced multimodal cognitive skills.

There was a lack of a benchmark evaluating sophisticated cognitive skills requiring expert-level knowledge, intricate logical reasoning, and precise visual perception within research-level scientific scenarios.
results: Proprietary models, with Gemini-2.5-Pro leading at 64.30% overall accuracy, significantly outperform open-source LMMs (best: InternVL-3-78B-Instruct at 38.80%), highlighting a notable gap in advanced scientific reasoning capabilities.

Quantitative Reasoning consistently yielded the lowest scores across all models (e.g., Gemini-2.5-Pro at 50.61%, best open-source at 22.04%), often performing near random chance for many open-source models.

Error analysis of top proprietary models revealed that incorrect visual perception (70.68%), inaccurate reasoning progress (63.25%), and lack of domain knowledge (49.40%) are primary failure modes, emphasizing the need for targeted architectural and training improvements.
solution: The team collected 241 research-grade experimental videos from the Journal of Visualized Experiments (JoVE), leveraging their tri-modal alignment (video, audio, manuscript) across four foundational scientific disciplines.

A meticulous multi-stage, semi-automatic annotation pipeline was developed, using specialized LLM agents (e.g., QA Generator, Visual Comparer) collaboratively guided and validated by human PhD-level domain experts.

The benchmark comprises 1,000 challenging multiple-choice questions categorized into Quantitative, Hypothetical, and Conceptual Reasoning, designed to require research-level knowledge and explicit video-based perception.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the University of Central Florida, UNC Chapel Hill, and Stanford University introduced SciVideoBench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating large multimodal models (LMMs) on scientific video reasoning. This benchmark reveals that current LMMs exhibit substantial limitations in performing expert-level scientific reasoning tasks, particularly those involving precise quantitative analysis from video content.

title: SciVideoBench: Benchmarking Scientific Video Reasoning in Large Multimodal Models

abstract: Spatial understanding over continuous visual input is crucial for MLLMs to evolve into general-purpose assistants in physical environments. Yet there is still no comprehensive benchmark that holistically assesses the progress toward this goal. In this work, we introduce MMSI-Video-Bench, a fully human-annotated benchmark for video-based spatial intelligence in MLLMs. It operationalizes a four-level framework, Perception, Planning, Prediction, and Cross-Video Reasoning, through 1,106 questions grounded in 1,278 clips from 25 datasets and in-house videos. Each item is carefully designed and reviewed by 3DV experts with explanatory rationales to ensure precise, unambiguous grounding. Leveraging its diverse data sources and holistic task coverage, MMSI-Video-Bench also supports three domain-oriented sub-benchmarks (Indoor Scene Perception Bench, Robot Bench and Grounding Bench) for targeted capability assessment. We evaluate 25 strong open-source and proprietary MLLMs, revealing a striking human--AI gap: many models perform near chance, and the best reasoning model lags humans by nearly 60%. We further find that spatially fine-tuned models still fail to generalize effectively on our benchmark. Fine-grained error analysis exposes systematic failures in geometric reasoning, motion grounding, long-horizon prediction, and cross-video correspondence. We also show that typical frame-sampling strategies transfer poorly to our reasoning-intensive benchmark, and that neither 3D spatial cues nor chain-of-thought prompting yields meaningful gains. We expect our benchmark to establish a solid testbed for advancing video-based spatial intelligence.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Current state-of-the-art MLLMs exhibit a substantial performance gap of nearly 60% compared to human ability on video-based spatial intelligence tasks, indicating significant room for improvement.

MLLMs struggle across all categories of spatial intelligence, including Spatial Construction, Motion Understanding, Planning, Prediction, and Cross-Video Reasoning, with Prediction tasks being the most challenging.

Neither providing more visual information (Sufficient-Coverage) nor using Chain-of-Thought prompting consistently improved MLLM performance, and attempts to integrate 3D spatial cues or use spatially fine-tuned models did not generalize effectively.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for multimodal large language models (MLLMs) lack comprehensive evaluation of spatial intelligence from continuous video inputs, often focusing on discrete images or limited aspects of spatial understanding.

Many current benchmarks rely on automatic question generation, which can lead to limited diversity, biases, and susceptibility to template overfitting, thereby hindering the assessment of true reasoning capabilities.

There is a need for a rigorous, holistic benchmark that can assess a full spectrum of spatial cognitive abilities, including perception, planning, prediction, and complex cross-video reasoning, crucial for developing embodied AI assistants.
results: The best-performing MLLM, Google’s Gemini 3 Pro, achieved only 38.0% accuracy on MMSI-Video-Bench, while human performance averaged 96.4%.

A detailed error analysis showed that Geometric Reasoning Errors were the most prevalent, particularly in Spatial Construction tasks, highlighting persistent struggles with inferring basic spatial relations.

Uniform frame sampling significantly outperformed consecutive sampling or adaptive keyframe sampling, demonstrating that broad temporal coverage is crucial for complex spatial and temporal reasoning in video-based tasks.
solution: Developed MMSI-Video-Bench, a multi-level benchmark featuring 1,106 fully human-annotated multiple-choice questions designed by 3DV experts, covering five main categories of spatial intelligence.
Curated a diverse dataset of approximately 20,000 video clips from 25 public sources and newly recorded in-house videos, ensuring broad coverage of real-world scenarios (indoor, outdoor, robotics).
Established a multi-stage cross-evaluation protocol with 100% approval from reviewing researchers to ensure high-quality, unambiguous, and challenging questions requiring non-trivial reasoning.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Shanghai AI Laboratory and several universities developed MMSI-Video-Bench, a holistic and human-annotated benchmark for video-based spatial intelligence. The benchmark evaluates multimodal large language models (MLLMs) across perception, planning, prediction, and cross-video reasoning tasks, revealing that current MLLMs achieve only 38.0% accuracy compared to 96.4% for human performance.

title: MMSI-Video-Bench: A Holistic Benchmark for Video-Based Spatial Intelligence

abstract:
Recent advances in CoT reasoning and RL post-training have been reported to
enhance video reasoning capabilities of MLLMs. This progress naturally raises a
question: can these models perform complex video reasoning in a manner
comparable to human experts? However, existing video benchmarks primarily
evaluate visual perception and grounding abilities, with questions that can be
answered based on explicit prompts or isolated visual cues. Such benchmarks do
not fully capture the intricacies of real-world reasoning, where humans must
actively search for, integrate, and analyze multiple clues before reaching a
conclusion. To address this issue, we present Video-Holmes, a benchmark
inspired by the reasoning process of Sherlock Holmes, designed to evaluate the
complex video reasoning capabilities of MLLMs. Video-Holmes consists of 1,837
questions derived from 270 manually annotated suspense short films, which spans
seven carefully designed tasks. Each task is constructed by first identifying
key events and causal relationships within films, and then designing questions
that require models to actively locate and connect multiple relevant visual
clues scattered across different video segments. Our comprehensive evaluation
of state-of-the-art MLLMs reveals that, while these models generally excel at
visual perception, they encounter substantial difficulties with integrating
information and often miss critical clues. For example, the best-performing
model, Gemini-2.5-Pro, achieves an accuracy of only 45%, with most models
scoring below 40%. We aim that Video-Holmes can serve as a "Holmes-test" for
multimodal reasoning, motivating models to reason more like humans and
emphasizing the ongoing challenges in this field. The benchmark is released in
this https URL

keyInsights: Current MLLMs face substantial challenges in complex video reasoning, with the best model reaching only 45% accuracy.
Active information seeking, cross-modal integration (visual and audio), and causal inference are major bottlenecks for current models.
Chain-of-Thought prompting's effectiveness is strongly correlated with the base model's intrinsic reasoning capabilities, sometimes amplifying errors in weaker models.
originalProblem: Existing multimodal language model (MLLM) benchmarks primarily evaluate basic visual perception and simple temporal reasoning tasks.
There is a gap in evaluating complex reasoning capabilities for real-world video understanding, such as active information seeking, cross-segment integration, and causal inference.
Current evaluation frameworks often provide explicit visual prompts, failing to assess the deductive reasoning processes required for intricate scenarios.
results: The best-performing MLLM, Gemini-2.5-Pro, achieved only 45% accuracy on the Video-Holmes benchmark, with most models scoring below 40%.
Models with enhanced thinking strategies, like Gemini-2.0-Flash-Thinking, significantly outperformed their vanilla counterparts.
Audio information proved crucial for performance, particularly in social reasoning tasks, indicating the importance of multimodal input beyond just vision.
solution: Introduces Video-Holmes, a benchmark comprising 270 suspense short films from YouTube, manually annotated for complex narrative elements and visual clues.
Defines seven distinct reasoning task categories, including Social Reasoning, Physical Anomaly Reasoning, and Temporal Causal Inference, to comprehensively evaluate video understanding.
Establishes an evaluation protocol requiring models to generate step-by-step reasoning processes, actively identify clues across segments, and provide explanations for their choices.
paper_summary.summary: The Video-Holmes benchmark introduces a 'Holmes-test' for multimodal reasoning in videos, challenging models to actively seek visual clues, integrate information, and perform complex causal inference across multiple video segments. State-of-the-art MLLMs achieved only 45% accuracy, revealing limitations in current models' complex video reasoning capabilities.

title: Video-Holmes: Can MLLM Think Like Holmes for Complex Video Reasoning?

abstract: Recent advancements in Large Video Language Models (LVLMs) have highlighted their potential for multi-modal understanding, yet evaluating their factual grounding in videos remains a critical unsolved challenge. To address this gap, we introduce Video SimpleQA, the first comprehensive benchmark tailored for factuality evaluation in video contexts. Our work differs from existing video benchmarks through the following key features: 1) Knowledge required: demanding integration of external knowledge beyond the video's explicit narrative; 2) Multi-hop fact-seeking question: Each question involves multiple explicit facts and requires strict factual grounding without hypothetical or subjective inferences. We also include per-hop single-fact-based sub-QAs alongside final QAs to enable fine-grained, stepby-step evaluation; 3) Short-form definitive answer: Answers are crafted as unambiguous and definitively correct in a short format with minimal scoring variance; 4) Temporal grounded required: Requiring answers to rely on one or more temporal segments in videos, rather than single frames. We extensively evaluate 33 state-of-the-art LVLMs and summarize key findings as follows: 1) Current LVLMs exhibit notable deficiencies in factual adherence, with the best-performing model o3 merely achieving an F-score of 66.3%; 2) Most LVLMs are overconfident in what they generate, with self-stated confidence exceeding actual accuracy; 3) Retrieval-augmented generation demonstrates consistent improvements at the cost of additional inference time overhead; 4) Multi-hop QA demonstrates substantially degraded performance compared to single-hop sub-QAs, with first-hop object or event recognition emerging as the primary bottleneck. We position Video SimpleQA as the cornerstone benchmark for video factuality assessment, aiming to steer LVLM development toward verifiable grounding in real-world contexts.
title: Video SimpleQA: Towards Factuality Evaluation in Large Video Language Models

abstract: Recent advances in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have demonstrated substantial potential in video understanding. However, existing benchmarks fail to comprehensively evaluate synergistic reasoning capabilities across audio and visual modalities, often neglecting either one of the modalities or integrating them in a logically inconsistent manner. To bridge this gap, we introduce OmniVideoBench, a large-scale and rigorously designed benchmark dedicated to assessing synergistic audio-visual understanding, with a strong emphasis on modality complementarity and logical consistency. Specifically, OmniVideoBench comprises 1000 high-quality question-answer(QA) pairs, each annotated with step-by-step reasoning traces, derived from 628 diverse videos ranging from several seconds to 30 minutes, and manually verified to guarantee complete correctness and uniqueness. Moreover, OmniVideoBench encompasses 13 carefully designed question types, covering temporal reasoning, spatial localization, counting, causal inference, summarization, and beyond, thereby capturing the essential challenges of video understanding. Evaluation of multiple MLLMs on OmniVideoBench reveals a pronounced gap between model performance and human reasoning, with open-source models lagging significantly behind their closed-source counterparts, underscoring the inherent difficulty of genuine audio-visual reasoning. We will release OmniVideoBench to foster the development of MLLMs with stronger and more generalizable reasoning capabilities.
title: OmniVideoBench: Towards Audio-Visual Understanding Evaluation for Omni MLLMs

abstract: In recent years, vision language models (VLMs) have made significant advancements in video understanding. However, a crucial capability - fine-grained motion comprehension - remains under-explored in current benchmarks. To address this gap, we propose MotionBench, a comprehensive evaluation benchmark designed to assess the fine-grained motion comprehension of video understanding models. MotionBench evaluates models' motion-level perception through six primary categories of motion-oriented question types and includes data collected from diverse sources, ensuring a broad representation of real-world video content. Experimental results reveal that existing VLMs perform poorly in understanding fine-grained motions. To enhance VLM's ability to perceive fine-grained motion within a limited sequence length of LLM, we conduct extensive experiments reviewing VLM architectures optimized for video feature compression and propose a novel and efficient Through-Encoder (TE) Fusion method. Experiments show that higher frame rate inputs and TE Fusion yield improvements in motion understanding, yet there is still substantial room for enhancement. Our benchmark aims to guide and motivate the development of more capable video understanding models, emphasizing the importance of fine-grained motion comprehension. Project page: this https URL .

keyInsights: Motion-level understanding is foundational but overlooked in current video benchmarks
Existing compression methods use shallow fusion, limiting their effectiveness
Higher frame rates improve performance but don't fully solve motion understanding challenges
Deep fusion throughout the visual encoder helps preserve temporal information
originalProblem: Current VLMs struggle with fine-grained motion comprehension despite advances in video understanding
High computational costs limit the frame rates needed for detailed motion analysis
Existing benchmarks lack dedicated evaluation of motion-level perception capabilities
results: State-of-the-art VLMs achieve less than 60% accuracy on MotionBench
TE Fusion outperforms other compression methods across multiple benchmarks
Performance improves with higher frame rates but significant room for enhancement remains
Deep fusion shows particular advantages in high compression ratio scenarios
solution: Created MotionBench, a comprehensive benchmark with 8,052 questions covering 6 motion categories
Proposed Through-Encoder (TE) Fusion method for better video feature compression and representation
Released 5,000 annotated videos with detailed motion descriptions for model training
paper_summary.summary: This paper evaluates and improves fine-grained motion understanding capabilities in video language models (VLMs).

title: MotionBench: Benchmarking and Improving Fine-grained Video Motion Understanding for Vision Language Models

abstract: Understanding fine-grained temporal dynamics is crucial for multimodal video comprehension and generation. Due to the lack of fine-grained temporal annotations, existing video benchmarks mostly resemble static image benchmarks and are incompetent at evaluating models for temporal understanding. In this paper, we introduce TemporalBench, a new benchmark dedicated to evaluating fine-grained temporal understanding in videos. TemporalBench consists of ~10K video question-answer pairs, derived from ~2K high-quality human annotations detailing the temporal dynamics in video clips. As a result, our benchmark provides a unique testbed for evaluating various temporal understanding and reasoning abilities such as action frequency, motion magnitude, event order, etc. Moreover, it enables evaluations on various tasks like both video question answering and captioning, both short and long video understanding, as well as different models such as multimodal video embedding models and text generation models. Results show that state-of-the-art models like GPT-4o achieve only 38.5% question answering accuracy on TemporalBench, demonstrating a significant gap (~30%) between humans and AI in temporal understanding. Furthermore, we notice a critical pitfall for multi-choice QA where LLMs can detect the subtle changes in negative captions and find a centralized description as a cue for its prediction, where we propose Multiple Binary Accuracy (MBA) to correct such bias. We hope that TemporalBench can foster research on improving models' temporal reasoning capabilities. Both dataset and evaluation code will be made available.

keyInsights: Current state-of-the-art multimodal video models demonstrate a substantial performance gap (approximately 30 percentage points) compared to humans in fine-grained temporal understanding.

The proposed Multiple Binary Accuracy (MBA) metric effectively exposes model limitations by mitigating biases present in traditional multi-choice QA, showing a significant drop in reported accuracy for top models.
Increasing the number of input frames provides limited and saturating benefits for fine-grained temporal understanding, indicating inherent limitations in how models integrate temporal information.
originalProblem: Existing video understanding benchmarks often suffer from a 'single-frame bias,' allowing models to achieve high scores by analyzing static information without true temporal comprehension.
Many benchmarks exhibit 'language prior bias,' where large language models can perform well on QA tasks even without visual input, by exploiting linguistic patterns.
Annotations in previous datasets lack the fine-grained temporal details needed to evaluate subtle dynamics like action order, frequency, magnitude, or specific participants.
results: State-of-the-art models like GPT-4o and Qwen2-VL-72B achieved only 38.5% and 38.3% MBA accuracy, respectively, on fine-grained video QA, significantly below human performance of 67.9%.
The MBA metric reduced GPT-4o's performance from 75.7% (traditional binary accuracy) to 38.5%, confirming its effectiveness in identifying and correcting evaluation pitfalls.

Models struggled most with understanding 'action frequency' (counting repeated actions) and showed a substantial performance drop on long video understanding tasks, highlighting critical areas for improvement.
solution: A meticulously curated dataset of 2,000 diverse video clips with detailed, two-stage human-annotated positive captions emphasizing fine-grained temporal dynamics.
LLM-assisted generation and author filtering of challenging negative captions that differ from positives by subtle word-level or event-level temporal changes.
Introduction of the Multiple Binary Accuracy (MBA) metric to counter the 'centralized description' bias in multi-choice QA, decomposing questions into multiple binary choices for robust evaluation.
paper_summary.summary: TemporalBench introduces a new benchmark and evaluation methodology for multimodal video models, focusing on fine-grained temporal understanding. It reveals a significant gap between human (67.9% MBA) and state-of-the-art AI (38.5% MBA) performance, particularly in tasks requiring precise temporal reasoning and event counting.

title: TemporalBench: Benchmarking Fine-grained Temporal Understanding for Multimodal Video Models

abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have made significant strides in the field of video understanding in recent times. Nevertheless, existing video benchmarks predominantly rely on text prompts for evaluation, which often require complex referential language and diminish both the accuracy and efficiency of human model interaction in turn. To address this limitation, we propose V2P-Bench, a robust and comprehensive benchmark for evaluating the ability of LVLMs to understand Video Visual Prompts in human model interaction scenarios. V2P-Bench consists of 980 videos and 1172 well-structured high-quality QA pairs, each paired with manually annotated visual prompt frames. The benchmark spans three main tasks and twelve categories, thereby enabling fine-grained, instance-level evaluation. Through an in-depth analysis of current LVLMs, we identify several key findings: 1) Visual prompts are both more model-friendly and user-friendly in interactive scenarios than text prompts, leading to significantly improved model performance and enhanced user experience. 2) Models are reasonably capable of zero-shot understanding of visual prompts, but struggle with spatiotemporal understanding. Even o1 achieves only 71.8%, far below the human expert score of 88.3%, while most open-source models perform below 60%. 3) LVLMs exhibit pervasive Hack Phenomena in video question answering tasks, which become more pronounced as video length increases and frame sampling density decreases, thereby inflating performance scores artificially. We anticipate that V2P-Bench will not only shed light on these challenges but also serve as a foundational tool for advancing human model interaction and improving the evaluation of video understanding.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

title: V2P-Bench: Evaluating Video-Language Understanding with Visual Prompts for Better Human-Model Interaction

abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved considerable accuracy in Optical Character Recognition (OCR) from static images. However, their efficacy in video OCR is significantly diminished due to factors such as motion blur, temporal variations, and visual effects inherent in video content. To provide clearer guidance for training practical MLLMs, we introduce the MME-VideoOCR benchmark, which encompasses a comprehensive range of video OCR application scenarios. MME-VideoOCR features 10 task categories comprising 25 individual tasks and spans 44 diverse scenarios. These tasks extend beyond text recognition to incorporate deeper comprehension and reasoning of textual content within videos. The benchmark consists of 1,464 videos with varying resolutions, aspect ratios, and durations, along with 2,000 meticulously curated, manually annotated question-answer pairs. We evaluate 18 state-of-the-art MLLMs on MME-VideoOCR, revealing that even the best-performing model (Gemini-2.5 Pro) achieves an accuracy of only 73.7%. Fine-grained analysis indicates that while existing MLLMs demonstrate strong performance on tasks where relevant texts are contained within a single or few frames, they exhibit limited capability in effectively handling tasks that demand holistic video comprehension. These limitations are especially evident in scenarios that require spatio-temporal reasoning, cross-frame information integration, or resistance to language prior bias. Our findings also highlight the importance of high-resolution visual input and sufficient temporal coverage for reliable OCR in dynamic video scenarios.

keyInsights: Current state-of-the-art MLLMs are far from mastering video OCR, with the top-performing model achieving only 73.7% accuracy on MME-VideoOCR, highlighting the benchmark's difficulty and discriminative power.

MLLMs exhibit a critical weakness in integrating and synthesizing textual information distributed across multiple video frames, showing particularly poor performance in cross-frame text understanding tasks.

A pervasive issue is the MLLMs' tendency to over-rely on language priors, often "correcting" visually clear misspellings in video text to semantically plausible forms, indicating a lack of visual fidelity in character-level OCR.
originalProblem: MLLMs demonstrate high accuracy in static image OCR but experience significant performance drops when applied to video content due to dynamic complexities like motion blur and temporal variations.
Existing OCR benchmarks are primarily designed for static images or offer limited scope for video, failing to assess comprehensive understanding and reasoning over textual content across time.
There is a need for a comprehensive evaluation framework that can rigorously test MLLMs on spatio-temporal text association, cross-frame information integration, and text-based reasoning in videos.
results: The evaluation of 18 MLLMs showed that larger model scales generally correlated with higher accuracy, but token compression strategies for video processing often led to lower scores, indicating potential information loss.

Significant performance gaps were observed across task categories, with models struggling most in dynamic scene comprehension and temporal alignment tasks, achieving below 60% accuracy in Change Detection & Tracking and Text-Based Video Understanding.

Tasks requiring complex cross-frame text integration, such as "Trajectory Recognition," resulted in 0% accuracy across top models, demonstrating a severe limitation in handling distributed textual information over time.
solution: Developed MME-VideoOCR, a comprehensive benchmark consisting of 1,464 videos and 2,000 meticulously human-annotated question-answer pairs, covering 10 task categories, 25 specific tasks, and 44 diverse scenarios.

The benchmark was constructed using a multi-pronged approach, including reconstructing data from existing video QA datasets, manually collecting public videos, and uniquely generating AI videos to ensure broad diversity and controlled challenges.

A rigorous process involving manual annotation, two-stage expert verification, and a debiasing test was implemented to ensure high data quality, minimize biases, and enhance the benchmark's reliability.
paper_summary.summary: A new benchmark, MME-VideoOCR, evaluates Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) on their OCR-based capabilities within dynamic video scenarios. This benchmark, comprising 1,464 videos and 2,000 human-annotated QA pairs, reveals that state-of-the-art MLLMs achieve only up to 73.7% accuracy, particularly struggling with cross-frame information integration and exhibiting a strong language prior bias.

title: MME-VideoOCR: Evaluating OCR-Based Capabilities of Multimodal LLMs in Video Scenarios

abstract: Humans acquire knowledge through three cognitive stages: perceiving information, comprehending knowledge, and adapting knowledge to solve novel problems. Videos serve as an effective medium for this learning process, facilitating a progression through these cognitive stages. However, existing video benchmarks fail to systematically evaluate the knowledge acquisition capabilities in Large Multimodal Models (LMMs). To address this gap, we introduce Video-MMMU, a multi-modal, multi-disciplinary benchmark designed to assess LMMs' ability to acquire and utilize knowledge from videos. Video-MMMU features a curated collection of 300 expert-level videos and 900 human-annotated questions across six disciplines, evaluating knowledge acquisition through stage-aligned question-answer pairs: Perception, Comprehension, and Adaptation. A proposed knowledge gain metric, {\Delta}knowledge, quantifies improvement in performance after video viewing. Evaluation of LMMs reveals a steep decline in performance as cognitive demands increase and highlights a significant gap between human and model knowledge acquisition, underscoring the need for methods to enhance LMMs' capability to learn and adapt from videos.

keyInsights: LMMs show a consistent performance decline with increasing cognitive demand, with Adaptation tasks proving most challenging, indicating difficulty in flexibly applying learned knowledge.

Models perform better in qualitative disciplines (Art, Humanities) but struggle significantly in quantitative and analytical domains (Science, Engineering, Medicine) requiring complex visual and numerical reasoning.
While audio transcripts generally improve basic understanding, they can paradoxically decrease performance for some LMMs on complex knowledge adaptation tasks.
LMMs exhibit a high 'Right-to-Wrong' rate in knowledge acquisition, suggesting difficulty integrating new video information without compromising previously correct reasoning.
originalProblem: Existing video benchmarks primarily focus on basic visual understanding tasks, not the more complex process of knowledge acquisition from educational content.
Current knowledge-driven benchmarks assess pre-trained knowledge or reasoning on static inputs, lacking the capability to evaluate learning from dynamic, instructional videos.
There is no systematic framework to measure how effectively Large Multimodal Models truly acquire, retain, and apply new knowledge from video content in a human-like cognitive manner.
results: Human experts consistently outperformed LMMs, achieving an overall accuracy of 74.44%, compared to 65.78% for the top LMM (Claude-3.5-Sonnet).
On the challenging Adaptation track, human knowledge gain (∆knowledge) was 33.1%, significantly higher than GPT-4o (15.6%) and Claude-3.5-Sonnet (11.4%).

Error analysis identified 'Method Adaptation Error' as the most prevalent failure mode (64% of errors), highlighting LMMs' struggle in generalizing learned problem-solving strategies to novel contexts.
solution: Developed Video-MMMU, a benchmark consisting of 300 college-level educational videos across six professional disciplines, coupled with 900 human-annotated question-answer pairs.
Designed QA pairs aligned with three progressive cognitive stages: Perception (information extraction), Comprehension (concept understanding), and Adaptation (applying knowledge to novel scenarios).
Introduced ∆knowledge, a normalized metric (Acc_post - Acc_pre) / (100% - Acc_pre), to quantitatively assess the knowledge gain achieved by LMMs on Adaptation questions after video viewing.
paper_summary.summary: A new benchmark, Video-MMMU, evaluates Large Multimodal Models' ability to acquire and apply knowledge from multi-discipline professional videos across perception, comprehension, and adaptation cognitive stages. The study found human experts achieved 33.1% knowledge gain (∆knowledge), while the best LMMs like GPT-4o reached 15.6%, highlighting a substantial gap in applying learned concepts to novel problems.

title: Video-MMMU: Evaluating Knowledge Acquisition from Multi-Discipline Professional Videos

abstract: The advent of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has expanded AI capabilities to visual modalities, yet existing evaluation benchmarks remain limited to single-video understanding, overlooking the critical need for multi-video understanding in real-world scenarios (e.g., sports analytics and autonomous driving). To address this significant gap, we introduce MVU-Eval, the first comprehensive benchmark for evaluating Multi-Video Understanding for MLLMs. Specifically, our MVU-Eval mainly assesses eight core competencies through 1,824 meticulously curated question-answer pairs spanning 4,959 videos from diverse domains, addressing both fundamental perception tasks and high-order reasoning tasks. These capabilities are rigorously aligned with real-world applications such as multi-sensor synthesis in autonomous systems and cross-angle sports analytics. Through extensive evaluation of state-of-the-art open-source and closed-source models, we reveal significant performance discrepancies and limitations in current MLLMs' ability to perform understanding across multiple videos. The benchmark will be made publicly available to foster future research.

keyInsights: Multi-video understanding presents a significant challenge for current MLLMs, with even top models performing substantially below human levels, highlighting a large gap in capabilities.

MLLMs exhibit imbalanced performance across different tasks, struggling particularly with In-Context Learning and Retrieval-Augmented Generation, indicating difficulties in adapting reasoning strategies or effectively filtering relevant information.

While performance generally scales with model size, architectural design for efficient token handling and specialized input formats for distinct video streams are crucial for managing numerous videos and high resolutions without performance degradation.
originalProblem: Existing MLLM evaluation benchmarks predominantly focus on understanding individual videos, overlooking real-world multi-video scenarios.
Many practical applications require MLLMs to process and reason across multiple, often asynchronous, video sources (e.g., autonomous driving, sports analytics).
Current MLLMs lack dedicated evaluation to assess their capabilities in aggregating, correlating, and reasoning with information from diverse video streams.
results: The highest-performing closed-source model, Gemini 2.5 Pro, achieved an overall accuracy of 58.4% on MVU-Eval, significantly lower than human performance of 93.6%.
Some open-source models, such as Qwen2.5-VL-72B, demonstrated competitive performance with 57.1% accuracy, sometimes surpassing smaller closed-source counterparts.
Model performance consistently declined as the number of videos per question increased, reinforcing the inherent difficulty of aggregating and reasoning over more diverse visual sources.
solution: MVU-Eval introduces a benchmark with 1,824 question-answer pairs spanning 4,959 videos, evaluating eight core competencies in multi-video perception (e.g., object recognition, spatial understanding) and reasoning (e.g., knowledge-intensive reasoning, temporal reasoning).

A hybrid data collection pipeline combines automated generation with multiple rounds of MLLM-based leakage removal and difficulty filtering, followed by rigorous human verification to ensure high-quality and challenging data.
Models are evaluated in a zero-shot setting using accuracy, processing uniform frame samples from multiple videos (e.g., 32 frames per video) and following specific prompt templates for each task.
paper_summary.summary: The paper introduces MVU-Eval, the first comprehensive benchmark for multi-video understanding in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), designed to assess eight distinct perception and reasoning abilities. Evaluating state-of-the-art models on this benchmark revealed that even top performers like Gemini 2.5 Pro achieve 58.4% accuracy, significantly below human performance of 93.6%, demonstrating substantial challenges in cross-video correlation and reasoning.

title: MVU-Eval: Towards Multi-Video Understanding Evaluation for Multimodal LLMs

abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown strong performance in visual and audio understanding when evaluated in isolation. However, their ability to jointly reason over omni-modal (visual, audio, and textual) signals in long and complex videos remains largely unexplored. We introduce MMOU, a new benchmark designed to systematically evaluate multimodal understanding and reasoning under these challenging, real-world conditions. MMOU consists of 15,000 carefully curated questions paired with 9038 web-collected videos of varying length, spanning diverse domains and exhibiting rich, tightly coupled audio-visual content. The benchmark covers 13 fundamental skill categories, all of which require integrating evidence across modalities and time. All questions are manually annotated across multiple turns by professional annotators, ensuring high quality and reasoning fidelity. We evaluate 20+ state-of-the-art open-source and proprietary multimodal models on MMOU. The results expose substantial performance gaps: the best closed-source model achieves only 64.2% accuracy, while the strongest open-source model reaches just 46.8%. Our results highlight the challenges of long-form omni-modal understanding, revealing that current models frequently fail to apply even fundamental skills in long videos. Through detailed analysis, we further identify systematic failure modes and provide insights into where and why current models break.

keyInsights: Current MLLMs exhibit a substantial performance gap compared to human capabilities, with the best model (Gemini 2.5 Pro) scoring 64.2% accuracy against human annotators' 84.3%.

The benchmark successfully enforces true cross-modal reasoning, as unimodal and cascaded models perform significantly worse than end-to-end multimodal systems, confirming the necessity of joint audio-visual perception.

All evaluated models struggle with fundamental reasoning skills and long-horizon temporal understanding, showing a consistent degradation in accuracy when relevant evidence appears later in extended videos.
originalProblem: Existing MLLMs often struggle with joint reasoning across multiple modalities, especially when processing long, complex, and real-world video content.

Current multimodal benchmarks frequently omit or treat audio as auxiliary, focus on short video clips, or lack the scale and task diversity to assess comprehensive, long-term audio-visual understanding.
A lack of robust diagnostic tools exists to systematically evaluate MLLMs' ability to integrate omni-modal signals (visual, audio, textual) and identify specific reasoning weaknesses.
results: Human annotators achieved 84.3% accuracy, while Gemini 2.5 Pro scored 64.2% and the top open-source model, MiniCPM-o 4.5, achieved 46.8% overall accuracy on MMOU.

Modality-restricted models (e.g., vision-only 44%, audio-only 17.7%) and cascaded approaches (28.1-33.1%) demonstrated considerably lower performance, validating the benchmark's strict requirement for integrated audio-visual understanding.

Model accuracy consistently decreased as the temporal position of the supporting evidence shifted toward the end of long videos, highlighting a limitation in long-term context retention and temporal reasoning across all models.
solution: Introduced MMOU, a benchmark featuring 15,000 multiple-choice questions derived from 9,038 real-world videos averaging 11.8 minutes in length, designed to necessitate explicit integration of audio and visual information.

Established a taxonomy of 13 fundamental audio-visual reasoning skills, curated through a multi-stage, expert-driven annotation pipeline to ensure questions strictly require joint cross-modal understanding.

Evaluated over 20 state-of-the-art MLLMs, including both closed-source and open-source models, alongside modality-restricted, text-only, and cascaded baselines, using micro-averaged accuracy and a supplementary open-ended evaluation.
paper_summary.summary: NVIDIA and University of Maryland researchers developed MMOU, a benchmark with 15,000 questions from nearly 9,000 long, complex real-world videos, to evaluate multimodal large language models (MLLMs) on strictly joint audio-visual reasoning. Current MLLMs achieve up to 64.2% accuracy, significantly below human performance of 84.3%, highlighting challenges in temporal understanding and integrated cross-modal perception.

title: MMOU: A Massive Multi-Task Omni Understanding and Reasoning Benchmark for Long and Complex Real-World Videos

abstract: The rapid development of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has expanded their capabilities from image comprehension to video understanding. However, most of these MLLMs focus primarily on offline video comprehension, necessitating extensive processing of all video frames before any queries can be made. This presents a significant gap compared to the human ability to watch, listen, think, and respond to streaming inputs in real time, highlighting the limitations of current MLLMs. In this paper, we introduce StreamingBench, the first comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate the streaming video understanding capabilities of MLLMs. StreamingBench assesses three core aspects of streaming video understanding: (1) real-time visual understanding, (2) omni-source understanding, and (3) contextual understanding. The benchmark consists of 18 tasks, featuring 900 videos and 4,500 human-curated QA pairs. Each video features five questions presented at different time points to simulate a continuous streaming scenario. We conduct experiments on StreamingBench with 13 open-source and proprietary MLLMs and find that even the most advanced proprietary MLLMs like Gemini 1.5 Pro and GPT-4o perform significantly below human-level streaming video understanding capabilities. We hope our work can facilitate further advancements for MLLMs, empowering them to approach human-level video comprehension and interaction in more realistic scenarios.

organization_info.name: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

keyInsights: Current MLLMs exhibit substantial performance gaps compared to human understanding, particularly in omni-source and contextual understanding tasks.
Model performance is sensitive to video length, with declines noted for longer sequences, and struggles when processing redundant or misleading information.
Temporal reasoning remains a challenge, as models perform worse when critical information appears after the query timestamp or when explicit reference resolution is lacking.
originalProblem: Current Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are primarily evaluated on static images and offline videos, lacking comprehensive benchmarks for real-time streaming video understanding.

Existing video understanding benchmarks assume full video availability, which fundamentally differs from streaming scenarios where models must process partial information sequentially under temporal constraints.
Real-world applications such as live video conferencing and autonomous driving require MLLMs to comprehend dynamic, incoming video streams in real-time.
results: Even the top-performing MLLM, Gemini 1.5 Pro, achieves substantially lower accuracy than human benchmarks across all task categories in StreamingBench.

Models perform relatively better on real-time visual understanding tasks but struggle significantly with omni-source (e.g., multimodal alignment) and contextual understanding (e.g., causal reasoning) challenges.
The proactive output task reveals particular weaknesses, with most models failing to accurately identify relevant moments and provide timely responses according to instructions.
solution: StreamingBench is introduced as a comprehensive benchmark with 900 videos and 4,500 question-answer pairs designed to evaluate MLLMs on streaming video understanding.
The benchmark organizes evaluation tasks into three main categories: Real-Time Visual Understanding, Omni-Source Understanding, and Contextual Understanding.
