Data construction combines automated generation using GPT-4o for visual tasks with human curation for more complex omni-source and contextual tasks, ensuring quality and relevance.
paper_summary.summary: StreamingBench is presented as a new benchmark for evaluating Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) on streaming video understanding, addressing the limitations of existing benchmarks that focus on offline video analysis. The evaluation of 13 MLLMs reveals significant performance gaps between current models and human-level understanding, particularly in tasks requiring complex contextual and omni-source integration.

title: StreamingBench: Assessing the Gap for MLLMs to Achieve Streaming Video Understanding

abstract: Spatial reasoning has emerged as a critical capability for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), drawing increasing attention and rapid advancement. However, existing benchmarks primarily focus on single-step perception-to-judgment tasks, leaving scenarios requiring complex visual-spatial logical chains significantly underexplored. To bridge this gap, we introduce Video-MSR, the first benchmark specifically designed to evaluate Multi-hop Spatial Reasoning (MSR) in dynamic video scenarios. Video-MSR systematically probes MSR capabilities through four distinct tasks: Constrained Localization, Chain-based Reference Retrieval, Route Planning, and Counterfactual Physical Deduction. Our benchmark comprises 3,052 high-quality video instances with 4,993 question-answer pairs, constructed via a scalable, visually-grounded pipeline combining advanced model generation with rigorous human verification. Through a comprehensive evaluation of 20 state-of-the-art MLLMs, we uncover significant limitations, revealing that while models demonstrate proficiency in surface-level perception, they exhibit distinct performance drops in MSR tasks, frequently suffering from spatial disorientation and hallucination during multi-step deductions. To mitigate these shortcomings and empower models with stronger MSR capabilities, we further curate MSR-9K, a specialized instruction-tuning dataset, and fine-tune Qwen-VL, achieving a +7.82% absolute improvement on Video-MSR. Our results underscore the efficacy of multi-hop spatial instruction data and establish Video-MSR as a vital foundation for future research. The code and data will be available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Current state-of-the-art MLLMs exhibit significant performance drops, spatial disorientation, and hallucination when faced with multi-hop spatial reasoning tasks.

MSR proficiency is not solely an emergent property of model scale; generic enhancement strategies like Chain-of-Thought prompting often prove detrimental, and atomic capability training does not translate to long-horizon spatial reasoning.
Specialized, logically-grounded instruction-tuning data is crucial for fostering deep spatial reasoning, demonstrating substantial performance gains in complex MSR tasks.
originalProblem: Existing MLLM benchmarks primarily focus on "single-step perception-to-judgment" tasks, which only evaluate basic object recognition or simple spatial relationships.
The multi-hop spatial reasoning (MSR) capabilities of MLLMs, involving complex 2D/3D spatial relationships and logical chains, remained largely unbenchmarked.
There was a critical gap between MLLMs' superficial visual perception and their deep logical reasoning abilities, limiting their application in advanced embodied AI tasks.
results: State-of-the-art MLLMs achieved overall accuracies between 20% and 44% on Video-MSR, with Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct (43.92%) and GPT-4o (41.87%) being top performers, yet indicating significant MSR limitations.

Performance varied across tasks: Gemini-2.5-Flash excelled at Chain-based Reference Retrieval (66.33%) and Route Planning (61.21%) but performed poorly on Counterfactual Physical Deduction (24.77%) and Constrained Localization.

Fine-tuning models on the MSR-9K instruction-tuning dataset yielded substantial improvements, with Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct showing a +7.82% absolute gain in overall accuracy and a remarkable +48.62% improvement on the Route Planning task.
solution: Developed Video-MSR, a novel benchmark featuring four distinct multi-hop spatial reasoning tasks: Constrained Localization, Chain-based Reference Retrieval, Route Planning, and Counterfactual Physical Deduction.

Created a scalable data construction pipeline that combines automated video-QA generation with a dual-phase verification process (automated blind test and manual expert review) to produce 4,993 high-quality video-QA pairs.
Curated MSR-9K, a specialized instruction-tuning dataset of 8,369 MSR QA pairs, designed to enhance MLLMs' capacity for deep spatial reasoning.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Baidu Inc., in collaboration with Nanjing University, The University of Queensland, and Peking University, introduced Video-MSR, the first benchmark to evaluate multi-hop spatial reasoning (MSR) in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) using dynamic video. The study revealed that state-of-the-art MLLMs struggle significantly with complex spatial logic, but performance can be substantially improved through specialized instruction tuning with the MSR-9K dataset.

title: Video-MSR: Benchmarking Multi-hop Spatial Reasoning Capabilities of MLLMs

abstract: While generative video models have achieved remarkable visual fidelity, their capacity to internalize and reason over implicit world rules remains a critical yet under-explored frontier. To bridge this gap, we present RISE-Video, a pioneering reasoning-oriented benchmark for Text-Image-to-Video (TI2V) synthesis that shifts the evaluative focus from surface-level aesthetics to deep cognitive reasoning. RISE-Video comprises 467 meticulously human-annotated samples spanning eight rigorous categories, providing a structured testbed for probing model intelligence across diverse dimensions, ranging from commonsense and spatial dynamics to specialized subject domains. Our framework introduces a multi-dimensional evaluation protocol consisting of four metrics: \textit{Reasoning Alignment}, \textit{Temporal Consistency}, \textit{Physical Rationality}, and \textit{Visual Quality}. To further support scalable evaluation, we propose an automated pipeline leveraging Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) to emulate human-centric assessment. Extensive experiments on 11 state-of-the-art TI2V models reveal pervasive deficiencies in simulating complex scenarios under implicit constraints, offering critical insights for the advancement of future world-simulating generative models.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Current state-of-the-art TI2V models, despite their impressive visual fidelity, systematically struggle with implicit world reasoning, particularly in logical and complex experiential scenarios.
There is a significant performance gap between closed-source and open-source models, with open-source models exhibiting more visual artifacts and lower reasoning capabilities.
The LMM-based judging pipeline, validated through ablation studies, demonstrates robust alignment with human preferences, enabling scalable and reliable evaluation for reasoning in video generation.
originalProblem: Existing video generation benchmarks predominantly emphasize surface-level qualities such as perceptual realism and temporal consistency, lacking diagnostic granularity for higher-level cognitive reasoning.
The capacity of current Text-Image-to-Video (TI2V) models to internalize and reason over implicit world rules (e.g., physical laws, social norms, logical inferences) remains largely unquantified.

Without a dedicated framework, models might generate visually convincing but physically implausible, logically inconsistent, or experientially nonsensical videos, hindering the development of true 'world simulators'.
results: The top-performing TI2V model, Hailuo 2.3, achieved only 22.5% accuracy on the RISE-Video benchmark, indicating pervasive deficiencies in implicit reasoning across all 11 evaluated models.
Models performed best on low-level Perceptual Knowledge tasks but showed consistently low performance in Logical Capability, highlighting a fundamental limitation in abstract, rule-based reasoning.
Sora 2 showed notable strength in Temporal Consistency, while Hailuo 2.3 demonstrated a clear advantage in Reasoning Alignment, outperforming the second-ranked model by 6.6% in this metric.
solution: Introduced RISE-Video, a reasoning-oriented benchmark featuring 467 human-annotated samples across eight distinct reasoning categories, from perceptual to logical knowledge.

Developed a multi-dimensional evaluation protocol using four complementary metrics: Reasoning Alignment, Temporal Consistency, Physical Rationality, and Visual Quality, with specialized handling for abstract tasks.
Proposed an automated and scalable LMM-based judging pipeline, primarily utilizing GPT-5, to efficiently assess videos while maintaining high alignment with human judgments.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Shanghai Jiao Tong University and Tencent Youtu Lab developed RISE-Video, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating the implicit world reasoning capabilities of Text-Image-to-Video (TI2V) models. This framework, which leverages an LMM-based automated judging pipeline, revealed that even leading TI2V models like Hailuo 2.3 achieved only 22.5% accuracy, consistently struggling with higher-level reasoning despite their visual fidelity.

title: RISE-Video: Can Video Generators Decode Implicit World Rules?

abstract: The recent rapid advancement of Text-to-Video (T2V) generation technologies are engaging the trained models with more world model ability, making the existing benchmarks increasingly insufficient to evaluate state-of-the-art T2V models. First, current evaluation dimensions, such as per-frame aesthetic quality and temporal consistency, are no longer able to differentiate state-of-the-art T2V models. Second, event-level temporal causality-an essential property that differentiates videos from other modalities-remains largely unexplored. Third, existing benchmarks lack a systematic assessment of world knowledge, which are essential capabilities for building world models. To address these issues, we introduce VideoVerse, a comprehensive benchmark focusing on evaluating whether the current T2V model could understand complex temporal causality and world knowledge to synthesize videos. We collect representative videos across diverse domains and extract their event-level descriptions with inherent temporal causality, which are then rewritten into text-to-video prompts by independent annotators. For each prompt, we design ten evaluation dimensions covering dynamic and static properties, resulting in 300 prompts, 815 events, and 793 evaluation questions. Consequently, a human preference-aligned QA-based evaluation pipeline is developed by using modern vision-language models to systematically benchmark leading open- and closed-source T2V systems, revealing the current gap between T2V models and desired world modeling abilities.
title: VideoVerse: Does Your T2V Generator Have World Model Capability to Synthesize Videos?

abstract: Existing video benchmarks often resemble image-based benchmarks, with question types like "What actions does the person perform throughout the video?" or "What color is the woman's dress in the video?" For these, models can often answer by scanning just a few key frames, without deep temporal reasoning. This limits our ability to assess whether large vision-language models (LVLMs) can truly think with videos rather than perform superficial frame-level analysis. To address this, we introduce GLIMPSE, a benchmark specifically designed to evaluate whether LVLMs can genuinely think with videos. Unlike prior benchmarks, GLIMPSE emphasizes comprehensive video understanding beyond static image cues. It consists of 3,269 videos and over 4,342 highly visual-centric questions across 11 categories, including Trajectory Analysis, Temporal Reasoning, and Forensics Detection. All questions are carefully crafted by human annotators and require watching the entire video and reasoning over full video context-this is what we mean by thinking with video. These questions cannot be answered by scanning selected frames or relying on text alone. In human evaluations, GLIMPSE achieves 94.82% accuracy, but current LVLMs face significant challenges. Even the best-performing model, GPT-o3, reaches only 66.43%, highlighting that LVLMs still struggle to move beyond surface-level reasoning to truly think with videos.

keyInsights: Current state-of-the-art Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) fundamentally struggle with tasks requiring deep temporal reasoning, precise quantitative estimation, and handling the full complexity of long video sequences.

The significant performance gap between LVLMs (top 66.43% for GPT-o3) and humans (94.82%) on GLIMPSE indicates that models often "glimpse" at key moments but fail to "think" holistically about video dynamics.
Video-finetuned LVLMs generally outperform image-based LVLMs on GLIMPSE, validating the benchmark's design and the necessity of specialized video training for temporal understanding.
originalProblem: Existing video understanding benchmarks often resemble image-based evaluations, allowing models to answer questions by examining key frames rather than requiring deep temporal reasoning across the entire video.

Current LVLM evaluations do not adequately assess complex temporal relationships, object trajectories, or subtle changes over time, potentially leading to an inflated perception of models' video comprehension abilities.

There was a lack of benchmarks specifically designed with "visual-centric" questions that demand comprehensive, multi-faceted reasoning over dynamic video content, exposing whether LVLMs truly understand videos or just perform superficial analysis.
results: Human performance on GLIMPSE achieved 94.82% accuracy, significantly outperforming the best-performing commercial LVLM, GPT-o3, which reached 66.43% accuracy.

Temporal Reasoning was the most challenging category for all models, with GPT-o3 scoring only 53.07%; the bidirectional question format exposed inconsistent understanding, with GPT-4o's accuracy dropping from 52.30% to 28.80% on question pairs.

Accuracy for Scene Context Awareness generally improved with longer video durations, while Temporal Reasoning accuracy declined for videos longer than 30 seconds, indicating struggles with processing and reasoning over extended temporal sequences.
solution: Developed GLIMPSE, a benchmark comprising 3,269 videos and 4,342 "visual-centric" question-answer pairs, meticulously curated to demand comprehensive analysis of dynamic visual elements across multiple frames.
The benchmark organizes questions into 11 distinct categories, each targeting a specific type of video-specific visual task, emphasizing deep reasoning content over superficial pattern recognition.

Implemented a novel bidirectional question format for "yes/no" temporal reasoning questions, requiring models to correctly answer paired questions (e.g., "A before B?" and "B before A?") to mitigate guessing bias and ensure true understanding of temporal order.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from UNC Chapel-Hill, Microsoft, and other institutions introduce GLIMPSE, a new benchmark designed to evaluate Large Vision-Language Models' (LVLMs) ability to perform deep video understanding. Evaluations on GLIMPSE reveal that current state-of-the-art LVLMs, with the best model achieving 66.43% accuracy, primarily perform superficial frame-level analysis and struggle significantly with true temporal reasoning and complex video dynamics, contrasting with human performance of 94.82%.

title: GLIMPSE: Do Large Vision-Language Models Truly Think With Videos or Just Glimpse at Them?

abstract:
From image to video understanding, the capabilities of Multi-modal LLMs
(MLLMs) are increasingly powerful. However, most existing video understanding
benchmarks are relatively short, which makes them inadequate for effectively
evaluating the long-sequence modeling capabilities of MLLMs. This highlights
the urgent need for a comprehensive and integrated long video understanding
benchmark to assess the ability of MLLMs thoroughly. To this end, we propose
ALLVB (ALL-in-One Long Video Understanding Benchmark). ALLVB's main
contributions include: 1) It integrates 9 major video understanding tasks.
These tasks are converted into video QA formats, allowing a single benchmark to
evaluate 9 different video understanding capabilities of MLLMs, highlighting
the versatility, comprehensiveness, and challenging nature of ALLVB. 2) A fully
automated annotation pipeline using GPT-4o is designed, requiring only human
quality control, which facilitates the maintenance and expansion of the
benchmark. 3) It contains 1,376 videos across 16 categories, averaging nearly 2
hours each, with a total of 252k QAs. To the best of our knowledge, it is the
largest long video understanding benchmark in terms of the number of videos,
average duration, and number of QAs. We have tested various mainstream MLLMs on
ALLVB, and the results indicate that even the most advanced commercial models
have significant room for improvement. This reflects the benchmark's
challenging nature and demonstrates the substantial potential for development
in long video understanding.

keyInsights: Automated Q&A generation using large language models like GPT-4o, coupled with multi-stage quality control, provides a scalable and reliable method for constructing large-scale, complex benchmarks.

Current state-of-the-art MLLMs, even commercial models, demonstrate significant limitations in deep comprehension and reasoning over ultra-long video contexts, especially for fine-grained visual and precise temporal tasks.
MLLM performance on long videos is sensitive to both increasing video length and limited input frame count, indicating a need for improved long-context processing and temporal modeling strategies.
originalProblem: Existing video understanding benchmarks primarily focus on short videos, inadequately assessing MLLMs' long-sequence modeling capabilities for complex narratives and temporal dependencies over extended periods.
Prior long video benchmarks rely on labor-intensive manual annotations, which limits their scalability, number of videos, average duration, and Q&A density.
The absence of a unified standard for question design in existing long video benchmarks creates inconsistencies, hindering objective and comparable evaluation of MLLMs.
results: ALLVB stands as the largest long video understanding benchmark, featuring 1,376 movies with an average duration of 114.62 minutes and a total of 252,420 multiple-choice Q&As.

Evaluation of mainstream MLLMs on ALLVB revealed Claude 3.5 Sonnet achieved the highest average accuracy at 75.2%, followed by GPT-4o at 68.0%, indicating a substantial performance gap for long video understanding tasks.

Models performed significantly worse on tasks requiring fine-grained visual attention and precise temporal understanding, such as Object Detection and Tracking, Temporal Action Localization, and the challenging Needle-in-a-Haystack task (e.g., GPT-4o 37.6%).
solution: Developed ALLVB, a comprehensive, integrated, and challenging benchmark for MLLMs' long video understanding, comprising 1,376 movies averaging nearly two hours.
Implemented a novel, fully automated annotation pipeline driven by GPT-4o, combined with rigorous multi-stage quality control, to generate 252,420 high-quality, multiple-choice Q&As.

Integrated 9 major video understanding tasks and 91 diverse sub-tasks into a unified Q&A format, including a novel "Needle-in-a-Haystack" task to test fine-grained visual retrieval and reasoning in long contexts.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from National University of Defense Technology and Hunan University introduced ALLVB, an "All-in-One Long Video Understanding Benchmark," designed to evaluate Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) on extended video content. This benchmark features 1,376 movies, averaging nearly two hours in duration, with 252,420 automatically generated Q&As, revealing that even state-of-the-art MLLMs like Claude 3.5 Sonnet (75.2% accuracy) and GPT-4o (68.0% accuracy) exhibit significant performance gaps in complex long-form video understanding tasks, particularly those requiring fine-grained detail retrieval.

title: ALLVB: All-in-One Long Video Understanding Benchmark

abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have made rapid progress in perception, understanding, and reasoning, yet existing benchmarks fall short in evaluating these abilities under continuous and dynamic real-world video streams. Such settings require models to maintain coherent understanding and reasoning as visual scenes evolve over time. **We introduce RTV-Bench, a fine-grained benchmark for real-time video analysis with MLLMs**. It is built upon three key principles: multi-timestamp question answering, hierarchical question structures spanning perception and reasoning, and multi-dimensional evaluation of continuous perception, understanding, and reasoning. RTV-Bench comprises 552 diverse videos and 4,608 carefully curated QA pairs covering a wide range of dynamic scenarios. We evaluate a broad range of state-of-the-art MLLMs, including proprietary, open-source offline, and open-source real-time models. Our results show that real-time models generally outperform offline counterparts but still lag behind leading proprietary systems. While scaling model capacity generally yields performance gains, simply increasing the density of sampled input frames does not consistently translate into improved results. These observations suggest inherent limitations in current architectures when handling long-horizon video streams, underscoring the need for models explicitly designed for streaming video processing and analysis.
title: RTV-Bench: Benchmarking MLLM Continuous Perception, Understanding and Reasoning through Real-Time Video

abstract: Existing benchmarks often highlight the remarkable performance achieved by state-of-the-art Multimodal Foundation Models (MFMs) in leveraging temporal context for video understanding. However, how well do the models truly perform visual temporal reasoning? Our study of existing benchmarks shows that this capability of MFMs is likely overestimated as many questions can be solved by using a single, few, or out-of-order frames. To systematically examine current visual temporal reasoning tasks, we propose three principles with corresponding metrics: (1) Multi-Frame Gain, (2) Frame Order Sensitivity, and (3) Frame Information Disparity. Following these principles, we introduce TOMATO, Temporal Reasoning Multimodal Evaluation, a novel benchmark crafted to rigorously assess MFMs' temporal reasoning capabilities in video understanding. TOMATO comprises 1,484 carefully curated, human-annotated questions spanning six tasks (i.e., action count, direction, rotation, shape & trend, velocity & frequency, and visual cues), applied to 1,417 videos, including 805 self-recorded and -generated videos, that encompass human-centric, real-world, and simulated scenarios. Our comprehensive evaluation reveals a human-model performance gap of 57.3% with the best-performing model. Moreover, our in-depth analysis uncovers more fundamental limitations beyond this gap in current MFMs. While they can accurately recognize events in isolated frames, they fail to interpret these frames as a continuous sequence. We believe TOMATO will serve as a crucial testbed for evaluating the next-generation MFMs and as a call to the community to develop AI systems capable of comprehending human world dynamics through the video modality.

keyInsights: Existing temporal reasoning benchmarks significantly overestimate MFM capabilities, exhibiting low Multi-Frame Gain (avg. <5%) and Frame Order Sensitivity (avg. <8%), in contrast to TOMATO's 66.3-81.0% and 34.1% respectively.
State-of-the-art MFMs struggle to interpret frames as continuous sequences, often over-rely on common sense, and are highly susceptible to noisy information, even with explicit instructions.

Models incorporating time-aware positional encoding (e.g., Qwen2-VL's M-RoPE) consistently show superior performance, suggesting architectural considerations are crucial for effective temporal reasoning.
originalProblem: Existing visual temporal reasoning benchmarks for Multimodal Foundation Models (MFMs) allow models to use 'shortcuts', such as relying on static information from single or out-of-order frames, leading to an overestimation of their true temporal reasoning capabilities.
A lack of rigorous, quantitative principles to assess how effectively a benchmark truly evaluates visual temporal reasoning, hindering accurate diagnosis of MFM limitations.

MFMs demonstrate impressive performance on current video understanding tasks, but their ability to genuinely interpret dynamic visual information over time, essential for AGI, remains unclear and potentially inflated.
results: The best-performing MFM, Qwen2-VL-72B, achieved only 37.9% accuracy on TOMATO, representing a 57.3% gap compared to human performance (95.2%), indicating substantial limitations.
MFMs show performance improvements from 1 to 8 frames, but accuracy plateaus beyond 8 frames, demonstrating an inability to effectively leverage additional temporal information.

MFMs exhibit significant performance drops on counterfactual scenarios (e.g., GPT-4o -37.7%, Qwen2-VL-72B -29.4%), confirming an over-reliance on pre-trained common-sense knowledge rather than visual evidence.
solution: Proposing three principled metrics: Multi-Frame Gain, Frame Order Sensitivity, and Frame Information Disparity, to quantitatively evaluate the rigor of visual temporal reasoning benchmarks.

Developing TOMATO (TempOral Reasoning MultimodAl EvaluaTiOn), a novel, human-annotated multiple-choice benchmark with 1,484 questions across six temporal reasoning tasks, meticulously designed to prevent shortcut-taking.
Conducting a comprehensive evaluation of 31 state-of-the-art MFMs on TOMATO, alongside in-depth error analyses to diagnose fundamental shortcomings in temporal understanding.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Yale University and the Allen Institute for AI introduce TOMATO, a new benchmark designed to rigorously assess visual temporal reasoning in multimodal foundation models. The study reveals that existing benchmarks overestimate model capabilities by allowing shortcuts, while TOMATO demonstrates current models achieve only 37.9% accuracy, compared to 95.2% for humans, revealing fundamental limitations in understanding continuous visual sequences.

title: TOMATO: Assessing Visual Temporal Reasoning Capabilities in Multimodal Foundation Models

abstract:
The sequential structure of videos poses a challenge to the ability of
multimodal large language models (MLLMs) to locate multi-frame evidence and
conduct multimodal reasoning. However, existing video benchmarks mainly focus
on understanding tasks, which only require models to match frames mentioned in
the question (hereafter referred to as "question frame") and perceive a few
adjacent frames. To address this gap, we propose MMR-V: A Benchmark for
Multimodal Deep Reasoning in Videos. The benchmark is characterized by the
following features. (1) Long-range, multi-frame reasoning: Models are required
to infer and analyze evidence frames that may be far from the question frame.
(2) Beyond perception: Questions cannot be answered through direct perception
alone but require reasoning over hidden information. (3) Reliability: All tasks
are manually annotated, referencing extensive real-world user understanding to
align with common perceptions. (4) Confusability: Carefully designed distractor
annotation strategies to reduce model shortcuts. MMR-V consists of 317 videos
and 1,257 tasks. Our experiments reveal that current models still struggle with
multi-modal reasoning; even the best-performing model, o4-mini, achieves only
52.5% accuracy. Additionally, current reasoning enhancement strategies
(Chain-of-Thought and scaling test-time compute) bring limited gains. Further
analysis indicates that the CoT demanded for multi-modal reasoning differs from
it in textual reasoning, which partly explains the limited performance gains.
We hope that MMR-V can inspire further research into enhancing multi-modal
reasoning capabilities.

keyInsights: Current MLLMs demonstrate a significant 33.5% accuracy gap compared to humans (52.5% vs 86%), indicating substantial limitations in deep video reasoning
Traditional reasoning enhancement strategies (Chain-of-Thought, scaling test-time compute) provide minimal improvements, suggesting current approaches are inadequate for multimodal video reasoning

Error analysis reveals "lack of visual reasoning" as the primary failure mode, with models relying on text-based reasoning from question frames rather than integrating long-range visual evidence across multiple frames
originalProblem: Existing video benchmarks primarily assess understanding tasks that require only superficial perception or matching adjacent frames, failing to evaluate deep multimodal reasoning capabilities
Current MLLMs lack robust evaluation for their ability to infer hidden information, analyze implications, and perform long-range temporal reasoning in video contexts
Limited benchmarks exist that challenge models to go beyond direct perception and engage in human-like cognitive processes for video understanding
results: Best-performing model (o4-mini) achieved only 52.5% accuracy, with open-source models lagging further behind (Gemma-3-27b-it highest at lower performance)
Chain-of-Thought prompting showed minimal gains (0.57% average), while "thinking" models provided only 2.4% improvement over baseline performance
Models performed better on implicit tasks (+7.9% average) than explicit tasks, with particular struggles in counterintuitive reasoning, sequential structure analysis, and comment matching categories
Increasing frame count improved performance with diminishing returns, while audio input provided consistent but small improvements (1.0-1.4%) across supporting models
solution: Created MMR-V benchmark with 1,257 manually annotated multiple-choice tasks across 317 YouTube videos requiring multi-frame analysis over long temporal distances

Categorized tasks into implicit reasoning (metaphor understanding, emotion recognition, theme interpretation) and explicit reasoning (causal analysis, sequential structure understanding) based on cognitive science principles
Employed sophisticated distractor generation using GPT-4o incorrect answers and human annotation to ensure high-quality, confusing options that prevent model shortcuts
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the Chinese Academy of Sciences and Tsinghua University develop MMR-V, a benchmark evaluating multimodal large language models' ability to perform deep reasoning in videos through 1,257 multiple-choice tasks across 317 YouTube videos (average 277 seconds), revealing substantial limitations in current MLLMs with the best-performing model (o4-mini) achieving only 52.5% accuracy compared to humans' 86%, organized into implicit reasoning categories (metaphor understanding, emotion recognition, theme interpretation) and explicit reasoning tasks (causal analysis, sequential structure understanding) that require analyzing evidence across temporally distant frames rather than adjacent-frame perception, with systematic error analysis showing that models primarily rely on text-based reasoning from question frames rather than integrating long-range visual evidence, while traditional reasoning enhancement strategies like Chain-of-Thought prompting provide minimal gains (0.57% average improvement) and scaling test-time compute offers only 2.4% improvement, demonstrating a critical gap between current AI capabilities and human-like multimodal understanding in dynamic video environments.

title: MMR-V: What's Left Unsaid? A Benchmark for Multimodal Deep Reasoning in  Videos

abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) hold promise for accelerating scientific discovery by interpreting complex experimental procedures. However, their true capabilities are poorly understood, as existing benchmarks neglect the fine-grained and long-horizon nature of authentic laboratory work, especially in wet-lab settings. To bridge this gap, we introduce ExpVid, the first benchmark designed to systematically evaluate MLLMs on scientific experiment videos. Curated from peer-reviewed video publications, ExpVid features a new three-level task hierarchy that mirrors the scientific process: (1) Fine-grained Perception of tools, materials, and actions; (2) Procedural Understanding of step order and completeness; and (3) Scientific Reasoning that connects the full experiment to its published conclusions. Our vision-centric annotation pipeline, combining automated generation with multi-disciplinary expert validation, ensures that tasks require visual grounding. We evaluate 19 leading MLLMs on ExpVid and find that while they excel at coarse-grained recognition, they struggle with disambiguating fine details, tracking state changes over time, and linking experimental procedures to scientific outcomes. Our results reveal a notable performance gap between proprietary and open-source models, particularly in high-order reasoning. ExpVid not only provides a diagnostic tool but also charts a roadmap for developing MLLMs capable of becoming trustworthy partners in scientific experimentation.

keyInsights: Proprietary MLLMs like GPT-5 and Gemini-2.5 consistently outperform open-source models and human baselines, especially in higher-order procedural understanding and scientific reasoning.

Current MLLMs struggle with fine detail disambiguation, tracking subtle state changes over time, and robustly linking visual experimental procedures to their broader scientific outcomes and discoveries.

While scaling improves performance in open-source models, 'thinking' strategies (e.g., chain-of-thought) do not consistently enhance video-grounded reasoning and can sometimes lead to reliance on logical priors rather than visual evidence.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) lack systematic evaluation on complex, real-world scientific experiment videos, especially for fine-grained and long-horizon tasks.
Existing video understanding benchmarks primarily focus on general human actions or outcome-focused medical scenarios, failing to address the unique challenges of 'wet-lab' scientific procedures.
Current knowledge-driven and scientific benchmarks often rely on static images or text, neglecting the temporal and visual complexities inherent in dynamic experimental videos.
A critical gap exists in systematically evaluating MLLMs' ability to perceive experimental manipulations, check procedural fidelity, and connect observations to scientific conclusions.
results: GPT-5 achieved the highest average score in Level-3 scientific reasoning tasks at 56.4%, with the best open-source model scoring only 39.6%, indicating a substantial performance gap.
Gemini-2.5-Flash (with 'think') led Level-1 perception tasks with 60.2% accuracy, surpassing the human baseline of 37.6% and the best open-source InternVL3-78B at 50.9%.

Ablation studies confirmed that visual inputs are indispensable, with performance significantly degrading (near zero for Level-3 tasks) without frames, while some models effectively leveraged up to 256 frames for improved accuracy.
solution: The team curated EXPVID using step-by-step experiment videos from JoVE, accompanied by ASR transcripts and peer-reviewed papers, ensuring high-quality visual and textual content.

They designed a three-level hierarchical task structure to evaluate MLLMs: fine-grained perception (Level-1), procedural understanding (Level-2), and scientific reasoning (Level-3) with 10 distinct tasks.
A vision-centric annotation pipeline, combining LLM assistance with rigorous multi-disciplinary expert validation, was employed to generate over 7,800 high-quality, vision-dependent QA pairs.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Shanghai AI Laboratory, Institute of Science Tokyo, and Nanjing University developed EXPVID, the first benchmark for scientific experiment video understanding and reasoning, leveraging JoVE videos and peer-reviewed papers. The benchmark evaluates Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) across perception, procedural understanding, and scientific reasoning tasks, revealing that proprietary models like GPT-5 and Gemini-2.5 significantly outperform open-source counterparts in complex scientific contexts.

title: ExpVid: A Benchmark for Experiment Video Understanding & Reasoning

abstract: While multimodal large language models (MLLMs) exhibit strong performance on single-video tasks (e.g., video question answering), their capability for spatiotemporal pattern reasoning across multiple videos remains a critical gap in pattern recognition research. However, this capability is essential for real-world applications, including multi-camera surveillance and cross-video procedural learning. To bridge this gap, we present CVBench, the first diagnostic benchmark designed to assess cross-video relational reasoning rigorously. CVBench comprises 1,000 question-answer pairs spanning three hierarchical tiers: cross-video object association (identifying shared entities), cross-video event association (linking temporal or causal event chains), and cross-video complex reasoning (integrating commonsense and domain knowledge). Built from five domain-diverse video clusters (e.g., sports, life records), the benchmark challenges models to analyze and integrate spatiotemporal patterns from dynamic visual streams. Extensive evaluation of 10+ leading MLLMs (including GPT-4o, Gemini-2.0-flash, Qwen2.5-VL) under zero-shot or chain-of-thought prompting paradigms. Key findings reveal stark performance gaps: even top models, such as GPT-4o, achieve only 63.5% accuracy on causal reasoning tasks, compared to the 91.3% accuracy of human performance. Crucially, our analysis reveals fundamental bottlenecks inherent in current MLLMs architectures, notably deficient inter-video context retention and poor disambiguation of overlapping entities. CVBench establishes a rigorous framework for advancing pattern recognition methodologies in multi-video scenarios, providing architectural insights for next-generation models. The data and evaluation code are available at: this https URL.

keyInsights: A substantial performance gap of 21.9 percentage points exists between the best-performing MLLM (Gemini-2.0-flash at 69.4%) and human intelligence (91.3%) in cross-video reasoning, indicating fundamental limitations in current MLLM architectures.

MLLMs exhibit pronounced weaknesses in tasks requiring precise temporal, causal, and spatial alignment across videos, such as multi-video temporal reasoning and cross-video causal reasoning, while showing relative competence in high-level semantic aggregation.

The benchmark diagnostics highlight the critical need for architectural improvements in MLLMs to enhance inter-video context retention, robust entity disambiguation, and sophisticated temporal-causal modeling across multiple video streams.
originalProblem: Current multimodal large language models (MLLMs) struggle with spatiotemporal pattern reasoning across multiple distinct video streams, often treating each video in isolation.

MLLMs lack robust mechanisms for inter-video context linking, persistent entity disambiguation across varying spatiotemporal contexts, and effective temporal-causal modeling across asynchronous video sequences.
Existing video understanding benchmarks primarily focus on single-video analysis, failing to specifically assess relational dynamics and reasoning across diverse multi-source video data.
results: Closed-source MLLMs (e.g., Gemini-2.0-flash, GPT-4o) consistently outperformed open-source models across all task categories, achieving up to 69.4% accuracy compared to the top open-source model's 59.4%.

Performance significantly degraded with input perturbations: a 4.5% drop when video order was randomized and a 9.3% drop when only single videos were presented, confirming the benchmark's focus on cross-video synergies.

Qualitative analysis revealed common failure modes including 'logical hallucination' (generating coherent but factually incorrect inferences) and 'cognitive depth deficits' (struggling with hierarchical and causal reasoning from multiple videos).
solution: CVBench pioneers a three-tiered diagnostic framework for cross-video relational reasoning, comprising 15 subtasks categorized into object association, event association, and complex reasoning.

A dataset of 1,000 multi-choice question-answer pairs was constructed from five diverse domains, utilizing an adaptive video segmentation, GPT-4o for segment-level captioning and relational summary annotation, and a two-stage human quality control process.

Over ten state-of-the-art MLLMs (both closed and open-source) were systematically evaluated using zero-shot or chain-of-thought prompting, with ablation studies performed on input perturbations, frame resolution, and video instance/duration, along with a human performance baseline.
paper_summary.summary: CVBench introduces the first diagnostic benchmark for evaluating cross-video relational pattern reasoning in multimodal large language models (MLLMs), revealing a significant 21.9% performance gap between current MLLMs and human intelligence across various object association, event association, and complex reasoning tasks. It provides a structured framework to assess how MLLMs integrate information, identify shared entities, and infer causal links across multiple, distinct video streams.

title: CVBench: Benchmarking Cross-Video Synergies for Complex Multimodal Reasoning

abstract: Human intelligence requires correctness and robustness, with the former being foundational for the latter. In video understanding, correctness ensures the accurate interpretation of visual content, and robustness maintains consistent performance in challenging conditions. Despite advances in video large language models (video LLMs), existing benchmarks inadequately reflect the gap between these models and human intelligence in maintaining correctness and robustness in video interpretation. We introduce the Video Thinking Test (Video-TT), to assess if video LLMs can interpret real-world videos as effectively as humans. Video-TT reflects genuine gaps in understanding complex visual narratives, and evaluates robustness against natural adversarial questions. Video-TT comprises 1,000 YouTube Shorts videos, each with one open-ended question and four adversarial questions that probe visual and narrative complexity. Our evaluation shows a significant gap between video LLMs and human performance.

keyInsights: Current Video LLMs, including top proprietary models, demonstrate a significant 'thinking gap' compared to human performance, particularly struggling with complex spatial-temporal reasoning, integrating world knowledge, and understanding intricate narratives.

Evaluating robustness with natural adversarial questions reveals current models are highly susceptible to misleading cues and inconsistent across varied question formulations, unlike humans who maintain high consistency.
Model performance saturates after processing approximately 8 frames, validating the benchmark's design in separating genuine understanding issues from limitations in frame sampling for long videos.
originalProblem: Existing video understanding benchmarks conflate models' genuine understanding capabilities with technical limitations in processing or sampling frames from long videos.

Current robustness evaluations rely on artificial perturbations (e.g., pixel alterations) rather than realistic, natural adversarial conditions, leading to an incomplete assessment of model reliability.
Benchmarks often fail to incorporate complex visual and narrative elements found in real-world videos, which require deep reasoning beyond simple recognition or temporal sequencing.
results: Humans achieve 84.3% accuracy and 64.3% robustness, while the best model (GPT-4o) reaches only 36.6% accuracy and 36.0% robustness on primary questions, indicating a substantial gap.
Models exhibit significantly higher accuracy on 'correctly-led' questions but a drastic performance drop on 'wrongly-led' questions, highlighting their vulnerability to misleading information.

Error analysis reveals models frequently struggle with spatial-temporal tasks (e.g., 79% for element localization, 88% for event counting) and tasks requiring world knowledge (44% error in character reaction/motivation).
solution: The Video Thinking Test (Video-TT) benchmark was developed using 1,000 YouTube Shorts videos, each with one primary open-ended question and four natural adversarial variants to assess correctness and robustness holistically.

Questions are explicitly designed to be answerable using only 80 uniformly sampled frames, ensuring that observed errors are attributable to genuine comprehension failures rather than insufficient frame sampling.
Complexity factors (visual and narrative) are systematically defined and integrated into question curation, ensuring the benchmark presents truly challenging scenarios requiring deep reasoning.
paper_summary.summary: The 'Video Thinking Test' (Video-TT) benchmark, developed by S-Lab at Nanyang Technological University, rigorously evaluates advanced video reasoning and understanding by disentangling genuine comprehension from frame sampling limitations. It introduces natural adversarial questions to assess robustness and reveals a significant performance gap between current Video LLMs and human intelligence, particularly in spatial-temporal reasoning and world knowledge application.

title: Towards Video Thinking Test: A Holistic Benchmark for Advanced Video Reasoning and Understanding

abstract: Most existing video understanding benchmarks for multimodal large language models (MLLMs) focus only on short videos. The limited number of benchmarks for long video understanding often rely solely on multiple-choice questions (MCQs). However, because of the inherent limitation of MCQ-based evaluation and the increasing reasoning ability of MLLMs, models can give the current answer purely by combining short video understanding with elimination, without genuinely understanding the video content. To address this gap, we introduce CG-Bench, a novel benchmark designed for clue-grounded question answering in long videos. CG-Bench emphasizes the model's ability to retrieve relevant clues for questions, enhancing evaluation credibility. It features 1,219 manually curated videos categorized by a granular system with 14 primary categories, 171 secondary categories, and 638 tertiary categories, making it the largest benchmark for long video analysis. The benchmark includes 12,129 QA pairs in three major question types: perception, reasoning, and hallucination. Compensating the drawbacks of pure MCQ-based evaluation, we design two novel clue-based evaluation methods: clue-grounded white box and black box evaluations, to assess whether the model generates answers based on the correct understanding of the video. We evaluate multiple closed-source and open-source MLLMs on CG-Bench. Results indicate that current models significantly underperform in understanding long videos compared to short ones, and a significant gap exists between open-source and commercial models. We hope CG-Bench can advance the development of more trustworthy and capable MLLMs for long video understanding. All annotations and video data are released at this https URL.

keyInsights: Existing MLLMs, even top commercial ones, show a substantial "credibility gap," often providing correct answers without genuinely grounding them in video evidence.
Temporal grounding, especially identifying precise clue intervals, is a major challenge for current models in long video contexts.
Including timestamps (frame and subtitle) is crucial for improving models' temporal grounding capabilities.
originalProblem: Most existing video understanding benchmarks focus on short video clips, failing to assess MLLMs' capabilities in processing and comprehending information across extended temporal contexts (long videos).

Multiple-choice question (MCQ) based evaluation for long videos allows MLLMs to deduce answers through superficial understanding or general knowledge, leading to inflated performance that doesn't reflect true comprehension.
There is a lack of mechanisms to evaluate if MLLMs can identify and ground their answers in specific, verifiable evidence (clues) within the long video, hindering trustworthiness assessment.
results: Closed-source models like GPT-4o (45.2% long-acc.) and Gemini-1.5 Pro (37.2% long-acc.) significantly outperform most open-source MLLMs on long-video MCQ tasks, yet still trail human performance (59.85%).
A major credibility gap was identified: GPT-4o's long-video MCQ accuracy (45.2%) dropped to 4.38% on Acc@IoU (which requires both correct answer and accurate clue grounding).
Top models like GPT-4o (77.5% CRR) and Gemini-1.5 Pro (74.3% CRR) show Clue Recovery Rate values below 100%, indicating struggles in retrieving short, relevant clues from long video contexts.
solution: Developed CG-Bench, a benchmark comprising over 1,200 manually curated long videos (10-80 minutes) to ensure diversity and avoid data contamination.
Annotated more than 12,000 "Question-Answer-Clue" (QAC) triplets, where each answer is supported by specific temporal intervals (clues) within the video.

Introduced novel credibility evaluation methods, including "white-box" (requiring explicit clue output) and "black-box" (Clue Recovery Rate) assessments, alongside a heuristic-based open-ended QA evaluation.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Nanjing University and Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory developed CG-Bench, a new benchmark for clue-grounded question answering in long videos (10-80 minutes) with over 12,000 question-answer-clue triplets. It introduces novel credibility evaluation metrics, revealing that state-of-the-art MLLMs struggle to ground their answers in specific video evidence despite achieving modest MCQ accuracy.

title: CG-Bench: Clue-grounded Question Answering Benchmark for Long Video Understanding

abstract: We introduce PerceptionComp, a manually annotated benchmark for complex, long-horizon, perception-centric video reasoning. PerceptionComp is designed so that no single moment is sufficient: answering each question requires multiple temporally separated pieces of visual evidence and compositional constraints under conjunctive and sequential logic, spanning perceptual subtasks such as objects, attributes, relations, locations, actions, and events, and requiring skills including semantic recognition, visual correspondence, temporal reasoning, and spatial reasoning. The benchmark contains 1,114 highly complex questions on 279 videos from diverse domains including city walk tours, indoor villa tours, video games, and extreme outdoor sports, with 100% manual annotation. Human studies show that PerceptionComp requires substantial test-time thinking and repeated perception steps: participants take much longer than on prior benchmarks, and accuracy drops to near chance (18.97%) when rewatching is disallowed. State-of-the-art MLLMs also perform substantially worse on PerceptionComp than on existing benchmarks: the best model in our evaluation, Gemini-3-Flash, reaches only 45.96% accuracy in the five-choice setting, while open-source models remain below 40%. These results suggest that perception-centric long-horizon video reasoning remains a major bottleneck, and we hope PerceptionComp will help drive progress in perceptual reasoning.

keyInsights: Complex video reasoning requires repeated perception and iterative evidence gathering, as demonstrated by humans achieving near-chance accuracy with a single viewing.
MLLM performance is sensitive to the density of perceptual input (frames) and the allocation of test-time reasoning computation.
Fine-grained perceptual errors, particularly spatial misunderstandings, often cascade and lead to reasoning failures in MLLMs.
originalProblem: Existing video benchmarks either offer simple perceptual tasks or rely on simplified visuals, inadequately testing complex, real-world visual reasoning in MLLMs.
Current benchmarks do not adequately assess multimodal intelligence in scenarios demanding sustained, iterative evidence gathering across temporally separated video segments.
There was no dedicated testbed for long-horizon, compositional video reasoning where fine-grained perception is the primary bottleneck.
results: State-of-the-art MLLMs, including Gemini-3-Flash, achieved a top accuracy of 45.96%, significantly below the 85.10% of ordinary humans with rewatching.
Human performance dropped to 18.97% (near chance) when only a single video viewing was allowed, empirically confirming the benchmark's "perception-bottlenecked" design.
MLLM accuracy consistently improved with more input frames and increased test-time reasoning budgets, though not all "thinking" models were robust to error amplification.
solution: Introduced PerceptionComp, a video benchmark with 1,114 manually annotated five-choice questions over 279 complex, real-world videos.
Questions are compositionally structured with multiple perceptual sub-conditions (e.g., semantic, spatial, temporal reasoning) and combine them using conjunctive or sequential logic.
Videos feature high scene and object complexity, compelling models to repeatedly gather and integrate fine-grained visual evidence across distinct temporal moments.
paper_summary.summary: PerceptionComp introduces a video benchmark designed to evaluate complex perception-centric reasoning, requiring multimodal large language models (MLLMs) to iteratively gather visual evidence from video segments. State-of-the-art MLLMs achieved up to 45.96% accuracy on this benchmark, while humans with unrestricted rewatching reached 85.10%, highlighting significant limitations in fine-grained visual understanding and multi-step evidence integration.

title: PerceptionComp: A Video Benchmark for Complex Perception-Centric Reasoning

abstract: We introduce MMVU, a comprehensive expert-level, multi-discipline benchmark for evaluating foundation models in video understanding. MMVU includes 3,000 expert-annotated questions spanning 27 subjects across four core disciplines: Science, Healthcare, Humanities & Social Sciences, and Engineering. Compared to prior benchmarks, MMVU features three key advancements. First, it challenges models to apply domain-specific knowledge and perform expert-level reasoning to analyze specialized-domain videos, moving beyond the basic visual perception typically assessed in current video benchmarks. Second, each example is annotated by human experts from scratch. We implement strict data quality controls to ensure the high quality of the dataset. Finally, each example is enriched with expert-annotated reasoning rationals and relevant domain knowledge, facilitating in-depth analysis. We conduct an extensive evaluation of 32 frontier multimodal foundation models on MMVU. The latest System-2-capable models, o1 and Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking, achieve the highest performance among the tested models. However, they still fall short of matching human expertise. Through in-depth error analyses and case studies, we offer actionable insights for future advancements in expert-level, knowledge-intensive video understanding for specialized domains.

keyInsights: Even top models (77.0% accuracy) significantly trail human expert performance (86.8%)
Chain-of-thought reasoning and System-2 capabilities notably improve model performance
Major failure modes include visual perception errors and domain knowledge application issues
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks lack comprehensive evaluation methods for specialized domain video understanding
No standardized way to assess how well AI models integrate domain expertise with visual comprehension
results: Successfully evaluated 32 frontier multimodal models across 3,000 expert-annotated questions
Established new quality standards for multimodal benchmarks through expert validation
Provided detailed error analysis framework for understanding model limitations
Created valuable resources for advancing specialized domain video understanding capabilities
solution: Created expert-validated video understanding benchmark across multiple disciplines
Developed systematic annotation process with 67 expert annotators and strict quality controls
Implemented detailed evaluation framework including reasoning rationales
paper_summary.summary: Yale researchers introduce MMVU, a groundbreaking benchmark for expert-level video understanding that spans 27 subjects across 4 disciplines, revealing significant gaps between current AI capabilities and human expert performance through 3,000 carefully curated expert-annotated questions and comprehensive evaluation of 32 frontier multimodal models.

title: MMVU: Measuring Expert-Level Multi-Discipline Video Understanding

abstract: Recent video multimodal large language models achieve impressive results across various benchmarks. However, current evaluations suffer from two critical limitations: (1) inflated scores can mask deficiencies in fine-grained visual understanding and reasoning, and (2) answer correctness is often measured without verifying whether models identify the precise spatio-temporal evidence supporting their predictions. To address this, we present VideoZeroBench, a hierarchical benchmark designed for challenging long-video question answering that rigorously verifies spatio-temporal evidence. It comprises 500 manually annotated questions across 13 domains, paired with temporal intervals and spatial bounding boxes as evidence. To disentangle answering generation, temporal grounding, and spatial grounding, we introduce a five-level evaluation protocol that progressively tightens evidence requirements. Experiments show that even Gemini-3-Pro correctly answers fewer than 17% of questions under the standard end-to-end QA setting (Level-3). When grounding constraints are imposed, performance drops sharply: No model exceeds 1% accuracy when both correct answering and accurate spatio-temporal localization are required (Level-5), with most failing to achieve any correct grounded predictions. These results expose a significant gap between surface-level answer correctness and genuine evidence-based reasoning, revealing that grounded video understanding remains a bottleneck for long-video QA. We further analyze performance across minimal evidence spans, atomic abilities, and inference paradigms, providing insights for future research in grounded video reasoning. The benchmark and code will be made publicly available.

keyInsights: Current video MLLMs exhibit a significant gap between generating correct answers and providing genuine evidence-based reasoning, often failing to leverage supporting spatio-temporal dynamics.
Locating key spatio-temporal evidence in long videos is a primary bottleneck for MLLMs; even with explicit evidence hints, models struggle with integrating localized cues and complex reasoning.

While agentic 'thinking-with-videos' paradigms and explicit visual input configurations show some benefits, current models are ultimately limited by the precision of evidence grounding rather than reasoning depth.
originalProblem: Existing video MLLM benchmarks produce inflated performance scores, potentially masking weaknesses in fine-grained visual understanding and complex reasoning.

Conventional evaluations assess only answer correctness, often failing to verify if model predictions are genuinely supported by precise spatio-temporal evidence from the video, which can lead to hallucination.
A lack of sufficiently challenging benchmarks for long videos that require fine-grained spatio-temporal reasoning and rigorously verify evidence grounding.
results: Frontier models like Gemini-3-Pro achieved only 17.0% accuracy on standard QA (Level-3) and a mere 1.0% on the strictest spatio-temporal grounding task (Level-5).

Performance consistently declined from Level-1 (with full hints) to Level-5 (requiring correct answer and accurate spatio-temporal grounding), with approximately two-thirds of evaluated models scoring zero at Level-5.

Atomic abilities such as small-object perception, spatial orientation discrimination, and counting tasks remained particularly challenging, with most models achieving less than 8% accuracy; human performance significantly outscored models (67.6% vs. 22.0%).
solution: Introduces VideoZeroBench, a dataset of 138 long, structurally complex videos (average 667.1 seconds) and 500 open-ended questions requiring precise, verifiable answers.
Employs LLM-assisted and human manual annotation to create questions with ground-truth temporal intervals and spatial bounding boxes to serve as explicit evidence.

Establishes a five-level hierarchical evaluation protocol that progressively tightens evidence requirements, from providing full hints to requiring correct answers with accurate spatio-temporal grounding (Level-5).
paper_summary.summary: VideoZeroBench introduces a hierarchical benchmark for long-video question answering that rigorously evaluates video MLLMs' ability to provide correct answers supported by precise spatio-temporal evidence. Evaluations on state-of-the-art models show low performance, particularly when requiring explicit evidence grounding, indicating challenges in fine-grained perception and reasoning.

title: VideoZeroBench: Probing the Limits of Video MLLMs with Spatio-Temporal Evidence Verification

abstract: Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) are improving rapidly, yet current Video Question Answering (VideoQA) benchmarks often admit single-cue shortcuts, under-testing reasoning that must integrate evidence across time. We introduce HERBench, a benchmark designed to make multi-evidence integration unavoidable: each question requires at least three non-overlapping cues drawn from distinct video segments. HERBench contains 26,806 five-way multiple-choice questions across 12 compositional tasks. To make evidential demand measurable, we introduce the Minimum Required Frame-Set (MRFS), the smallest number of frames a model must fuse to answer correctly, and show that HERBench imposes higher evidential demand than prior benchmarks. Evaluating 13 state-of-the-art Video-LLMs yields only 31-42% accuracy, only modestly above the 20\% random-guess baseline. We disentangle this failure into two critical bottlenecks: (1) a retrieval deficit, where frame selectors overlook key evidence, and (2) a fusion deficit, where models fail to integrate information even when all necessary evidence is provided. HERBench thus provides a principled benchmark for studying robust multi-evidence video understanding.
title: HERBench: A Benchmark for Multi-Evidence Integration in Video Question Answering

abstract:
Recent great advances in video generation models have demonstrated their
potential to produce high-quality videos, bringing challenges to effective
evaluation. Unlike human evaluation, existing automated evaluation metrics lack
high-level semantic understanding and reasoning capabilities for video, thus
making them infeasible and unexplainable. To fill this gap, we curate
GRADEO-Instruct, a multi-dimensional T2V evaluation instruction tuning dataset,
including 3.3k videos from over 10 existing video generation models and
multi-step reasoning assessments converted by 16k human annotations. We then
introduce GRADEO, one of the first specifically designed video evaluation
models, which grades AI-generated videos for explainable scores and assessments
through multi-step reasoning. Experiments show that our method aligns better
with human evaluations than existing methods. Furthermore, our benchmarking
reveals that current video generation models struggle to produce content that
aligns with human reasoning and complex real-world scenarios. The models,
datasets, and codes will be released soon.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) fine-tuning and the inclusion of complete reasoning steps (Overview, Description, Analysis, Assessment) are critical for MLLMs to perform human-like, explainable video evaluation.

Current Text-to-Video models consistently struggle with generating videos that exhibit high rationality (common sense) and creativity, highlighting a significant gap in their ability to produce complex, imaginative, or logically consistent content.

Specialized MLLM training for video evaluation, like GRADEO, substantially outperforms general-purpose MLLMs and traditional metrics in aligning with human judgments, especially for high-level semantic dimensions.
originalProblem: Existing automated metrics for Text-to-Video (T2V) generation lack high-level semantic understanding and struggle to correlate well with human judgment for complex attributes.
Human evaluation is the 'gold standard' but is costly, time-consuming, and unscalable for the rapid and continuous assessment required by T2V model development.

Generic Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are not specifically trained for fine-grained, multi-dimensional T2V evaluation, often lacking human alignment and the ability to provide detailed rationales.
results: GRADEO achieved the highest Spearman's rank correlation (SROCC) with human judgments across all seven dimensions (e.g., 0.743 for Quality, 0.797 for Creativity, 0.747 for Safety), significantly outperforming all baseline metrics and MLLMs.

It demonstrated superior performance in pairwise comparison accuracy on external datasets, such as 0.815 for T2VQA-DB and 0.840 for TVGE-Video Quality, further validating its alignment with human preferences.

Benchmarking of eight open-source T2V models using GRADEO-BENCH revealed that VideoCrafter2 had the best overall performance, while most models showed pronounced weaknesses in rationality and creativity dimensions.
solution: A robust evaluation framework was defined with seven dimensions, ranging from low-level perception (Quality, Aesthetic) to high-level reasoning (Rationality, Safety, Creativity), each with granular key aspects.

The GRADEO-Instruct dataset was constructed, comprising 3.3k videos and 16k human annotations, converted into a four-step Chain-of-Thought (CoT) instruction tuning format to teach multi-step reasoning.

GRADEO was developed by fine-tuning the Qwen2-VL-7B MLLM on GRADEO-Instruct, enabling it to perform human-like multi-step reasoning to generate both explainable scores and detailed textual assessments.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Tsinghua University and The Chinese University of Hong Kong introduced GRADEO, an automated evaluation model for text-to-video generation that uses multi-step reasoning. It leverages a human-annotated Chain-of-Thought dataset and achieves superior alignment with human judgments across seven dimensions, providing explainable scores to guide T2V model development.

title: GRADEO: Towards Human-Like Evaluation for Text-to-Video Generation via  Multi-Step Reasoning

abstract:
Large multimodal models (LMMs) have recently emerged as a powerful tool for
long video understanding (LVU), prompting the development of standardized LVU
benchmarks to evaluate their performance. However, our investigation reveals a
rather sober lesson for existing LVU benchmarks. First, most existing
benchmarks rely heavily on multiple-choice questions (MCQs), whose evaluation
results are inflated due to the possibility of guessing the correct answer;
Second, a significant portion of questions in these benchmarks have strong
priors to allow models to answer directly without even reading the input video.
For example, Gemini-1.5-Pro can achieve over 50\% accuracy given a random frame
from a long video on Video-MME. We also observe that increasing the number of
frames does not necessarily lead to improvement on existing benchmarks, which
is counterintuitive. As a result, the validity and robustness of current LVU
benchmarks are undermined, impeding a faithful assessment of LMMs' long-video
understanding capability. To tackle this problem, we propose VideoEval-Pro, a
realistic LVU benchmark containing questions with open-ended short-answer,
which truly require understanding the entire video. VideoEval-Pro assesses both
segment-level and full-video understanding through perception and reasoning
tasks. By evaluating 21 proprietary and open-source video LMMs, we conclude the
following findings: (1) video LMMs show drastic performance ($>$25\%) drops on
open-ended questions compared with MCQs; (2) surprisingly, higher MCQ scores do
not lead to higher open-ended scores on VideoEval-Pro; (3) compared to other
MCQ benchmarks, VideoEval-Pro benefits more from increasing the number of input
frames. Our results show that VideoEval-Pro offers a more realistic and
reliable measure of long video understanding, providing a clearer view of
progress in this domain.

keyInsights: Multiple-choice question (MCQ) performance does not reliably predict actual video understanding capabilities, with models showing substantial performance drops on open-ended questions.
A noticeable performance gap exists between proprietary and open-source large multimodal models on challenging open-ended long video understanding tasks.
Effective long video understanding genuinely requires processing temporal information, as performance on VideoEval-Pro consistently improved with increased frame count.
originalProblem: Current long video understanding benchmarks primarily use multiple-choice questions (MCQs), allowing models to exploit statistical biases or prior knowledge rather than demonstrate true video comprehension.
Many existing benchmark questions can be answered correctly using only single frames or common sense, failing to assess the temporal reasoning skills crucial for long video understanding.
MCQ scores on existing benchmarks do not correlate well with performance on more challenging, open-ended questions, suggesting an inflated perception of model capabilities.
results: Large multimodal models experienced substantial performance drops, exceeding 25%, when evaluated on VideoEval-Pro's open-ended format compared to traditional MCQ benchmarks.
GPT-4.1 achieved the highest accuracy among proprietary models, while Qwen2.5-VL led among open-source alternatives on the VideoEval-Pro benchmark.
Performance on VideoEval-Pro consistently improved as the number of input frames increased from 1 to 512, indicating a genuine benefit from more temporal information, unlike some existing benchmarks.
solution: The VideoEval-Pro benchmark reformulates existing multiple-choice questions from four major datasets into open-ended, short-answer format questions.
It requires models to generate free-form answers, forcing demonstration of actual comprehension and reasoning without relying on option elimination or statistical biases.
The evaluation framework employs GPT-4o as an LLM judge to assess answer correctness, providing a more nuanced evaluation than simple string matching.
paper_summary.summary: VideoEval-Pro introduces a new benchmark for long video understanding that converts existing multiple-choice questions into open-ended formats, revealing that large multimodal models perform substantially worse on open-ended tasks compared to traditional multiple-choice evaluations. The benchmark demonstrates that current models genuinely benefit from increased temporal information and struggle with holistic video understanding.

title: VideoEval-Pro: Robust and Realistic Long Video Understanding Evaluation

abstract: The inherent complexity of video understanding makes it difficult to attribute whether performance gains stem from visual perception, linguistic reasoning, or knowledge priors. While many benchmarks have emerged to assess high-level reasoning, the essential criteria that constitute video understanding remain largely overlooked. Instead of introducing yet another benchmark, we take a step back to re-examine the current landscape of video understanding. In this work, we provide Video-Oasis, a sustainable diagnostic suite designed to systematically evaluate existing evaluations and distill spatio-temporal challenges for video understanding. Our analysis reveals two critical findings: (1) 54% of existing benchmark samples are solvable without visual input or temporal context, and (2) on the remaining samples, state-of-the-art models exhibit performance barely exceeding random guessing. To bridge this gap, we investigate which algorithmic design choices contribute to robust video understanding, providing practical guidelines for future research. We hope our work serves as a standard guideline for benchmark construction and the rigorous evaluation of architecture development. Code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: A majority of existing video understanding benchmark tasks (54%) are solvable via shortcuts, where models do not require full video dependency, leading to inflated performance metrics.
When evaluated on distilled video-native challenges that truly require spatio-temporal reasoning, even advanced models perform barely above random chance, exposing fundamental limitations.

Key algorithmic improvements such as precise temporal localization, adaptive reasoning strategies, and specific Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) configurations are important for enhancing performance on genuine video understanding tasks.
originalProblem: It is challenging to attribute performance gains in multi-modal and video-specific large language models (Video-LLMs) to specific sources like visual perception, linguistic reasoning, or pre-existing world knowledge.
Many existing video understanding evaluations contain 'shortcuts,' allowing models to achieve high scores without genuinely processing visual input or temporal context.
There is a lack of unified criteria and systematic diagnostics for constructing and validating new video understanding benchmarks, leading to inconsistent assessment of models.
results: 54% of QA pairs across 14 benchmark datasets were identified as shortcuts, meaning they could be solved without complete video dependency, with models maintaining 30-50% accuracy under these conditions.

On the 11,332 distilled video-native QA pairs, state-of-the-art models, including proprietary ones, achieved accuracies mostly between 26% and 36%, only slightly exceeding the 25.63% random-guess baseline.

Integrating precise temporal localization improved performance on video-native samples (oracle experiment showed a 35.0% to 50.8% increase), and adaptive reasoning methods (Qwen3-VLvoting achieved 46.2%) approached the performance of leading proprietary models on these difficult tasks.
solution: A diagnostic suite, Video-Oasis (V-Oasis), was developed with Visual Dependency Tests (Blind, Audio, Narrative) and Temporal Dependency Tests (Center-Frame, Frame Shuffling, Bag-of-Frames) to assess task necessity for video-centric reasoning.
The framework incorporates human-in-the-loop verification to identify and filter ambiguous or redundant video question-answering (QA) samples.

V-Oasis was applied to 14 diverse benchmarks to quantify the prevalence of shortcuts, distill genuinely video-native challenges, and investigate algorithmic design choices (e.g., temporal localization, adaptive reasoning, training paradigms) for robust video understanding.
paper_summary.summary: Video-Oasis (V-Oasis) introduces a diagnostic framework to audit video understanding benchmarks, revealing that 54% of existing tasks can be solved without requiring genuine spatio-temporal video understanding. It demonstrates that current state-of-the-art models perform near random-chance on the distilled video-native challenges.

title: Video-Oasis: Rethinking Evaluation of Video Understanding

abstract: We introduce ReXTime, a benchmark designed to rigorously test AI models' ability to perform temporal reasoning within video events. Specifically, ReXTime focuses on reasoning across time, i.e. human-like understanding when the question and its corresponding answer occur in different video segments. This form of reasoning, requiring advanced understanding of cause-and-effect relationships across video segments, poses significant challenges to even the frontier multimodal large language models. To facilitate this evaluation, we develop an automated pipeline for generating temporal reasoning question-answer pairs, significantly reducing the need for labor-intensive manual annotations. Our benchmark includes 921 carefully vetted validation samples and 2,143 test samples, each manually curated for accuracy and relevance. Evaluation results show that while frontier large language models outperform academic models, they still lag behind human performance by a significant 14.3% accuracy gap. Additionally, our pipeline creates a training dataset of 9,695 machine generated samples without manual effort, which empirical studies suggest can enhance the across-time reasoning via fine-tuning.
title: ReXTime: A Benchmark Suite for Reasoning-Across-Time in Videos

abstract:
Despite rapid advances in vision-language models (VLMs), current benchmarks
for multimodal reasoning fall short in three key dimensions. First, they
overwhelmingly rely on static images, failing to capture the temporal
complexity of real-world environments. Second, they narrowly focus on
mathematical problem-solving, neglecting the broader spectrum of reasoning
skills -- including abstract, physical, planning, spatial, and temporal
capabilities -- required for robust multimodal intelligence. Third, many
benchmarks quickly saturate, offering limited headroom for diagnosing failure
modes or measuring continued progress. We introduce MORSE-500 (Multimodal
Reasoning Stress-test Environment), a video benchmark composed of 500 fully
scripted clips with embedded questions spanning six complementary reasoning
categories. Each instance is programmatically generated using deterministic
Python scripts (via Manim, Matplotlib, MoviePy), generative video models, and
curated real footage. This script-driven design allows fine-grained control
over visual complexity, distractor density, and temporal dynamics -- enabling
difficulty to be scaled systematically as models improve. Unlike static
benchmarks that become obsolete once saturated, MORSE-500 is built to evolve:
its controllable generation pipeline supports the creation of arbitrarily
challenging new instances, making it ideally suited for stress-testing
next-generation models. Initial experiments with state-of-the-art systems --
including various Gemini 2.5 Pro and OpenAI o3 which represent the strongest
available at the time, alongside strong open-source models -- reveal
substantial performance gaps across all categories, with particularly large
deficits in abstract and planning tasks. We release the full dataset,
generation scripts, and evaluation harness to support transparent,
reproducible, and forward-looking multimodal reasoning research.
